{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"The cyber security principles","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Purpose of the cyber security principles

The purpose of the cyber security principles is to provide strategic guidance on how an organisation can protect their information technology and operational technology systems from cyber threats. These cyber security principles are grouped into six functions:

- **Govern:** Develop and maintain a strong and resilient cyber security culture.
- **Identify:** Identify assets and associated security risks.
- **Protect:** Implement and maintain controls to manage security risks.
- **Detect:** Detect and analyse cyber security events to identify cyber security incidents.
- **Respond:** Respond to cyber security incidents.
- **Recover:** Resume normal business operations following cyber security incidents.

# Implementing the cyber security principles

Within each function, the cyber security principles are listed in a logical sequence that reflects how they should be considered for implementation. The numbers used for the cyber security principles are identifiers only and do not indicate an implementation order.

When implementing the cyber security principles, each cyber security principle should be supported by administrative and technical controls proportionate to an organisation’s size, complexity, operating environment and risk profile. These controls should be subject to ongoing risk-based monitoring and assurance activities. Where the term ‘systems’ is used, the elements listed in brackets define the scope of that cyber security principle.

Overall, the cyber security principles are interdependent and must be implemented collectively to achieve comprehensive cyber security outcomes."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-gove","sequence":150},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-iden","sequence":220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-prot","sequence":400},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-dete","sequence":460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-resp","sequence":520},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/cyber-security-principles-the-cyber-security-principles-reco","sequence":550}],"title":"The cyber security principles","version":"2026.06.18"}

{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cabling infrastructure","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Applicability

This section is only applicable to facilities located within Australia.

# Shared facilities

In addition to common controls, this section provides additional controls for shared facilities, such as a single floor, or part of a floor, within a multi-tenanted building.

# Cables and structured cabling systems

For the purposes of this section, a cable is defined as any fibre optic or copper material housed within a protective sheath for the purposes of transmitting data or control signals from one point in a facility to another. Each cable will form part of a structured cabling system and will need to comply with the Australian Standards associated with that system. In addition to network communications and data systems, some common building management structured cabling systems found within facilities are:

- fire control and sensor systems
- security control and surveillance systems
- lighting control systems
- access control systems
- voice and emergency telephony systems
- emergency control alert systems.

# Cable sheaths and conduits

A cable’s protective sheath is not considered a conduit.

# Cable connector types

The same cable connector types can be used for all systems within a facility regardless of their sensitivity or classification.

# Further information

[Australian cabling standards](#85c9b592-8734-4860-8243-ac0821f78854) can be obtained from the Australian Communications and Media Authority.

Further information on SCEC-approved tamper-evident seals can be found on the SCEC’s [Security Equipment Evaluated Products List](#f7e13f7b-eb48-4294-bd01-9c22c756d96b).

Further information on audio secure rooms can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3450},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3500},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3550},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3610},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3660},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3710},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3740},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3760},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastructure-cabling-infrast","sequence":3780},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-infrastruct As such, it is critical that Australian Signals Directorate (ASD)-approved cryptography is used."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-telephone-systems-cord","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0233","sequence":4420}],"title":"Cordless telephone systems","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Off-hook audio protection","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using off-hook protection features minimises the chance of background conversations being accidentally coupled into handsets, headsets and speakerphones. Limiting the time an active microphone is open minimises this security risk."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-telephone-systems-off-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0236","sequence":4460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0931","sequence":4470}],"title":"Off-hook audio protection","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Personnel awareness","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As there is a potential for unintended disclosure of information when using telephone systems, personnel should be made aware of the sensitivity or classification of information that may be discussed used them. In addition, personnel should also be made aware of the security risks associated with the use of non-secure telephone systems in areas where sensitive or classified conversations may occur.\n\nWhen using cryptographic equipment to enable different levels of conversation for different kinds of connections, providing a visual indication to personnel as to the sensitivity or classification of information that can be discussed over the telephone system can assist in reducing the likelihood of unintended disclosure of information."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-telephone-systems-pers","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0229","sequence":4360},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0230","sequence":4370},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0231","sequence":4380}],"title":"Personnel awareness","version":"2026.06.18"}

{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Video conferencing and Internet Protocol telephony","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Internet Protocol telephony

This section describes the controls applicable to Internet Protocol (IP) telephony and extends upon the ‘Telephone systems’ section of these guidelines.

# Video conferencing and Internet Protocol telephony gateways

Where a video conferencing or IP telephony network is connected to another network from a different security domain, the ‘Gateways’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b) applies.

# Further information

Further information on gateways can be found in the ‘Gateways’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on firewalls can be found in the ‘Firewalls’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on the use of web conferencing solutions can be found in ASD’s [Web conferencing security](#e19a0c40-0a68-4fba-aea1-142fdd57dc75) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4510},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4560},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4650},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4670},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4700},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-communications-systems-video-conferencing-and","sequence":4730}],"title":"Video conferencing and Internet Protocol telephony","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cryptographic algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# High assurance cryptographic algorithms

High assurance cryptographic algorithms, which are not covered in this section, can be used for the protection of SECRET and TOP SECRET data if they are suitably implemented in HACE. Further information on high assurance cryptographic algorithms can be obtained from ASD.

# ASD-Approved Cryptographic Algorithms

There is no guarantee of a cryptographic algorithm’s resistance to currently unknown attacks. However, the cryptographic algorithms listed in this section have been extensively scrutinised by industry and academic communities in a practical and theoretical setting. Approval for the use of the cryptographic algorithms listed in this section is limited to cases where they are implemented in accordance with these guidelines.

The approved asymmetric cryptographic algorithms are:

- Diffie-Hellman (DH) for agreeing on encryption session keys
- Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) for agreeing on encryption session keys
- Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA) for digital signatures
- Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm (ML-DSA) for digital signatures
- Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism (ML-KEM) for encapsulating encryption session keys (and similar keys)
- Rivest-Shamir-Adleman (RSA) for digital signatures and transporting encryption session keys (and similar keys).

The only approved hashing algorithm for general-purpose use is Secure Hashing Algorithm 2 (SHA-2). However, Secure Hashing Algorithm 3 (SHA-3), including its extendable-output functions (XOFs), is approved exclusively for use within ML-DSA and ML-KEM.

The only approved symmetric cryptographic algorithm is Advanced Encryption Standard (AES).

Where there is a range of key sizes for a cryptographic algorithm, some key sizes are not approved as they are insecure against current attacks or do not provide an adequate safety margin against possible future attacks. For example, advances in integer factorisation methods have rendered some RSA moduli sizes vulnerable and could render other RSA moduli vulnerable in the future.

The minimum targets used for the effective security strength of cryptographic algorithms listed within this section are:

- 112 bits for non-classified data
- 112 bits for OFFICIAL: Sensitive data
- 112 bits for PROTECTED data
- 128 bits for SECRET data
- 192 bits for TOP SECRET data.

Certain key sizes and parameters, such as specific elliptic curves, are preferred to promote interoperability with the United States’ National Security Agency’s [Commercial National Security Algorithm Suite 2.0](#e8a28fa5-4ea1-4ef5-a870-4d59e40fab37).

# Further information

Further information on planning for the transition to post-quantum cryptography can be found in ASD’s [Planning for post-quantum cryptography](#0b1a477d-b08a-4491-9cfc-c9d435b28dc8) publication.

Further information on post-quantum traditional hybrid schemes can be found in the United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Next steps in preparing for post-quantum cryptography](#b54cf0d1-d275-426e-98aa-9c678b9d55a5) guidance.

Further information on how to combine the different components of a post-quantum traditional hybrid scheme used for key encapsulation can be found in NIST SP 800-56C Rev. 2, [Recommendation for Key-Derivation Methods in Key-Establishment Schemes](#a75de67b-9502-47e9-b48b-5e2a45d528e1). This publication does not pertain to post-quantum traditional hybrid schemes used for digital signatures."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-c","sequence":14790},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-asymmet","sequence":14810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-d","sequence":14850},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-e","sequence":14870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-e","sequence":14910},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-t","sequence":14950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-p","sequence":14970},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-t","sequence":15020},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-t","sequence":15040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-r","sequence":15080},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-s","sequence":15120},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-s","sequence":15160},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-transit","sequence":15190},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-post-qu","sequence":15210}],"title":"Cryptographic algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Asymmetric cryptographic algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"ECDH is vulnerable to different types of attacks than DH. Consequently, ECDH offers more effective security per bit increase in key size than DH. This leads to smaller data requirements, which in turn means that the elliptic curve variants have become de facto global standards. For reduced data cost, and to promote interoperability, ECDH should be used in preference to DH where possible."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-asymmet","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0994","sequence":14800}],"title":"Asymmetric cryptographic algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Post-quantum traditional hybrid schemes","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A post-quantum traditional hybrid scheme is a multi-algorithm scheme where at least one cryptographic algorithm is a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm (e.g. ML-KEM) and at least one cryptographic algorithm is a traditional cryptographic algorithm (e.g. RSA). Generally, such schemes have the advantage of the security offered by the traditional cryptographic algorithm if the post-quantum cryptographic algorithm is vulnerable to an implementation flaw or new attack. This advantage comes at the cost of increased complexity, making maintenance, analysis and secure implementation more difficult, as well as having greater computational and bandwidth overheads.\n\nThe use of post-quantum traditional hybrid schemes is not recommended; however, it is not prohibited. If such schemes are to be used, at least one of the post-quantum or traditional cryptographic algorithms, or both, should be an AACA. It is important to note though, that in the presence of a CRQC, the security of such schemes is reduced to that provided by the post-quantum cryptographic algorithm. As such, there is no practical value in the use of such schemes in the presence of a CRQC. An organisation choosing to implement a post-quantum traditional hybrid scheme should also keep in mind the eventual additional cost of transitioning to a pure post-quantum scheme in the future."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-post-qu","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1996","sequence":15200}],"title":"Post-quantum traditional hybrid schemes","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using cryptographic algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When encryption is applied to data at rest, it provides an additional layer of defence against unauthorised access by malicious actors. In doing so, it is critical that an AACA or high assurance cryptographic algorithm is used. Furthermore, if cryptographic equipment, applications or libraries implement unapproved cryptographic algorithms, it is possible that these cryptographic algorithms could be used without a user’s knowledge. In combination with an assumed level of security confidence, this can represent a security risk. As such, an organisation should ensure that only AACAs or high assurance cryptographic algorithms can be used by disabling all unapproved cryptographic algorithms (preferred) or by advising users not to use the unapproved cryptographic algorithms via usage policies."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-c","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1080","sequence":14770},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0471","sequence":14780}],"title":"Using cryptographic algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When identifying a suitable curve from NIST SP 800-186, a base point order and key size of at least 224 bits for correctly implemented ECDH provides 112 bits of effective security strength, with larger key sizes providing more bits of effective security strength. Security of a curve selected from another source cannot be assumed to have the same security using base point order and key size alone.\n\nTaking into account projected technological advances in quantum computing, ECDH will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-e","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0474","sequence":14880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1761","sequence":14890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1762","sequence":14900}],"title":"Using Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using post-quantum cryptographic algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Post-quantum cryptographic algorithms are more complex than their traditional counterparts. To reduce the risk that vulnerabilities are introduced via implementation errors, approval is given to specific post-quantum cryptographic standards. In doing so, FIPS 204 and FIPS 203 state that other foundational standards must also be adhered to, including the required use of FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. However, while the use of FIPS 140-3 validated modules is preferred, it is not required."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-p","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1990","sequence":14960}],"title":"Using post-quantum cryptographic algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using Rivest-Shamir-Adleman","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A modulus of 2048 bits for correctly implemented RSA provides 112 bits of effective security strength, with larger modulus sizes providing more bits of effective security strength.\n\nTaking into account projected technological advances in quantum computing, RSA will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-r","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0476","sequence":15050},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1765","sequence":15060},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0477","sequence":15070}],"title":"Using Rivest-Shamir-Adleman","version":"2026.06.18"}

The ML-KEM standard contains three different parameter sets: ML-KEM-512, ML-KEM-768 and ML-KEM-1024. The use of ML-KEM-768 and ML-KEM-1024 are approved.\n\nFor interoperability and maintainability reasons, ML-KEM-768 will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-algorithms-using-t","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1995","sequence":15030}],"title":"Using the Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cryptographic fundamentals","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Purpose of cryptography

The purpose of cryptography is to provide confidentiality, integrity, authentication and non-repudiation of data. In doing so, confidentiality protects data by making it unreadable to all but authorised entities, integrity protects data from accidental or deliberate manipulation by entities, authentication ensures that an entity is who they claim to be, and non-repudiation provides proof that an entity performed a particular action.

# Using encryption

Encryption of data at rest can be used to protect sensitive or classified data stored on information technology (IT) equipment and media. In addition, encryption of data in transit can be used to protect sensitive or classified data communicated over public network infrastructure. However, when an organisation uses encryption for data at rest, or data in transit, they are not reducing the sensitivity or classification of the data; they are simply reducing the immediate consequences of the data being accessed by malicious actors.

# International standards for cryptographic modules

International Organization for Standardization (ISO)/International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) 19790:2025, [Information security, cybersecurity and privacy protection – Security requirements for cryptographic modules](#a07c439d-b2f4-4ee2-b56e-f17000f3722f), and ISO/IEC 24759:2025, [Information security, cybersecurity and privacy protection – Test requirements for cryptographic modules](#aaf83df2-02e3-4f9f-bdf5-33fbff42842d), are international standards for the design and validation of hardware and software cryptographic modules.

Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-3, [Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules](#32c8fa3a-45bb-4f88-baee-73a7696aa2ad) and National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Special Publication (SP) 800-140, [FIPS 140-3 Derived Test Requirements (DTR): CMVP Validation Authority Updates to ISO/IEC 24759](#197db872-c6a9-4b29-9888-1552e908252c) are United States standards based upon earlier versions of ISO/IEC 19790 and ISO/IEC 24759.

# Further information

Further information on cryptographic key management practices can be found in ASD’s [Managing cryptographic keys and secrets](#58589044-8b34-4d46-bf70-103ee8d10bc9) publication.

Further information on cryptographic key management practices for HACE is available from ASD.

Further information on general cryptographic key management practices can be found in NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5, [Recommendation for Key Management: Part 1 – General](#ffc8a1f6-f4e3-4ff5-a7f9-f41f766274eb).

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on evaluated products can be found in the ‘Evaluated product procurement’ section of the [Guidelines for evaluated products](#a699a3aa-828d-479b-b50b-98127bb19437).

Further information on the evaluation of cryptographic modules, including testing requirements, is available as part of the [Cryptographic Module Validation Program](#28cd91d2-e5cb-4896-bcfc-abb32e05e61d) which is jointly operated by NIST and the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security.

Further information on the evaluation of the implementation of cryptographic algorithms, including testing requirements, is available as part of the [Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Program](#42e7d99d-9709-4355-a54d-98959c552aa1) which is jointly operated by NIST and the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security.

Further information on the protection of IT equipment and media can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-commu","sequence":14570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-high-","sequence":14590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-crypt","sequence":14610},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-crypt","sequence":14660},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-data-","sequence":14680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-handl","sequence":14700},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-trans","sequence":14720},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-repor","sequence":14750}],"title":"Cryptographic fundamentals","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Communications security doctrine","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) specifies additional communications security requirements in Australian Communications Security Instructions and ASD Broadcasts that must be complied with when operating High Assurance Cryptographic Equipment (HACE). Such requirements supplement these guidelines and, where conflicts occur, take precedence."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-commu","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0499","sequence":14560}],"title":"Communications security doctrine","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cryptographic implementation assurance","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Securely implementing cryptographic algorithms and protocols is a difficult task that requires expertise and diligence. In doing so, suppliers, and their cyber supply chains, need to carry out their duties competently and honestly as small flaws in cryptographic equipment, applications or libraries can be catastrophic and difficult to detect. Therefore, to provide a degree of cryptographic implementation assurance, cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries should be assessed by one of the following processes, listed in order of preference:

- a Common Criteria evaluation against an ASD-endorsed Protection Profile
- a FIPS 140-3 cryptographic evaluation via the United States and Canada’s Cryptographic Module Validation Program
- a cryptographic evaluation via the United States and Canada’s Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Program
- a Common Criteria evaluation against an Evaluation Assurance Level
- an independent security review by a reputable process or body.

Where a suitable Common Criteria evaluated product does not exist, an alternate product capable of securely implementing ASD-Approved Cryptographic Algorithms (AACAs) and ASD-Approved Cryptographic Protocols (AACPs) may be used. However, cyber supply chain security risks still need to be considered to ensure the product does not present a high risk. For example, an organisation should still follow robust and secure procurement processes by selecting a product from a supplier with:

- a history of undertaking Common Criteria evaluations for their other products
- a demonstrated commitment to the security of their products and services, including by adopting Secure by Design principles and practices, responsively resolving known vulnerabilities, and providing clear end of life advice for consumers
- a demonstrated commitment to transparency for their products
- a strong track record of maintaining the security of their own systems
- sufficient information on how to securely configure their products."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-crypt","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0457","sequence":14620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0460","sequence":14630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0465","sequence":14640},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0467","sequence":14650}],"title":"Cryptographic implementation assurance","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Data recovery","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"To ensure that access to encrypted data is not lost due to the loss, damage or failure of an encryption key, it is important that where practical cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries provide a means of data recovery."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-data-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0455","sequence":14670}],"title":"Data recovery","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Handling encrypted IT equipment and media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When a user authenticates to the encryption functionality of IT equipment or media, encrypted data is made available. At such a time, the IT equipment or media should be handled according to its original sensitivity or classification. Once the user deauthenticates from the encryption functionality, such as shutting down a device or activating a lock screen, the IT equipment or media can be considered protected by the encryption functionality again."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-handl","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0462","sequence":14690}],"title":"Handling encrypted IT equipment and media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"High Assurance Cryptographic Equipment","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"To ensure interoperability and maintain trust, all HACE must be issued an Approval for Use by ASD and be operated in accordance with the latest version of their associated Australian Communications Security Instructions."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-high-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1802","sequence":14580}],"title":"High Assurance Cryptographic Equipment","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Reporting cryptographic-related cyber security incidents","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"If cryptographic equipment or associated keying material is compromised, or suspected of being compromised, then the confidentiality and integrity of previous and future communications may also be compromised. In such cases, the cyber security incident should be reported to the chief information security officer, or one of their delegates, as soon as possible after it occurs, and all keying material should be changed."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-repor","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0142","sequence":14730},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1091","sequence":14740}],"title":"Reporting cryptographic-related cyber security incidents","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Transporting cryptographic equipment","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Transporting cryptographic equipment in a keyed state may expose its keying material to potential compromise. Therefore, if cryptographic equipment is transported in a keyed state, it should be done based on the sensitivity or classification of its keying material."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-fundamentals-trans","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0501","sequence":14710}],"title":"Transporting cryptographic equipment","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cryptographic protocols","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# High assurance cryptographic protocols

High assurance cryptographic protocols, which are not covered in this section, can be used for the protection of SECRET and TOP SECRET data if they are suitably implemented in HACE. Further information on high assurance cryptographic protocols can be obtained from ASD.

# ASD-Approved Cryptographic Protocols

There is no guarantee of a protocol’s resistance to currently unknown attacks. However, the protocols listed in this section have been extensively scrutinised by industry and academic communities in a practical and theoretical setting. Approval for the use of the protocols listed in this section is limited to cases where they are implemented in accordance with these guidelines.

The AACPs are:

- Transport Layer Security (TLS)
- Secure Shell (SSH)
- Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension (S/MIME)
- OpenPGP Message Format
- Internet Protocol Security (IPsec)
- Wi-Fi Protected Access 2
- Wi-Fi Protected Access 3.

# Further information

Further information on AACPs can be found in the following sections of these guidelines.

Further information on the use of Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 and Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 can be found in the ‘Wireless networks’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-protocols","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-protocols-using-cr","sequence":15250}],"title":"Cryptographic protocols","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using cryptographic protocols","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When encryption is applied to data in transit, it provides an additional layer of defence against unauthorised access or manipulation by malicious actors. In doing so, it is critical that an AACP or high assurance cryptographic protocol is used. Furthermore, if cryptographic equipment, applications or libraries implement unapproved cryptographic protocols, it is possible that these cryptographic protocols could be used without a user’s knowledge. In combination with an assumed level of security confidence, this can represent a security risk. As such, an organisation should ensure that only AACPs or high assurance cryptographic protocols can be used by disabling all unapproved cryptographic protocols (preferred) or by advising users not to use the unapproved cryptographic protocols via usage policies."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-cryptographic-protocols-using-cr","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0469","sequence":15230},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0481","sequence":15240}],"title":"Using cryptographic protocols","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Internet Protocol Security","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Using Internet Protocol Security\n\nWhen using IT equipment or applications that implement IPsec, controls for using AACAs and AACPs in the ‘Cryptographic algorithms’ and ‘Cryptographic protocols’ sections of these guidelines will also need to be consulted."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-mode-","sequence":15550},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-proto","sequence":15570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-key-e","sequence":15590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-encry","sequence":15610},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-pseud","sequence":15630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-integ","sequence":15650},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-diffi","sequence":15670},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-secur","sequence":15690},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-perfe","sequence":15710}],"title":"Internet Protocol Security","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Diffie-Hellman groups","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A sufficiently large DH modulus provides greater security for key exchanges when establishing IPsec connections.\n\nTaking into account projected technological advances in quantum computing, DH and ECDH will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-diffi","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0999","sequence":15660}],"title":"Diffie-Hellman groups","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Encryption algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The only approved encryption algorithm for IPsec connections is AES. IKE version 2 supports the use of AES with Cipher Block Chaining, Counter Mode, Counter with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code, and Galois/Counter Mode. However, supported modes may vary between different cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-encry","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1771","sequence":15600}],"title":"Encryption algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Integrity algorithms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The approved integrity algorithms for IPsec connections are HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA384 and HMAC-SHA512. However, if using AES with Galois/Counter Mode as the encryption algorithm, it can also be used for authentication purposes. In such cases, the integrity algorithm should be configured as NONE.\n\nFor interoperability and maintainability reasons, HMAC-SHA256 will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-integ","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0998","sequence":15640}],"title":"Integrity algorithms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Key exchange","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"There are several methods for establishing shared keying material for IPsec connections, including manual keying and the IKE protocol. As the IKE protocol addresses several security risks associated with manual keying, it is the preferred method for key establishment. As IKE version 1 has been deprecated, IKE version 2 should be used."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-key-e","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1233","sequence":15580}],"title":"Key exchange","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Mode of operation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"IPsec can be operated in tunnel mode or transport mode. The tunnel mode of operation is preferred as it provides full encapsulation of IP packets while the transport mode of operation only encapsulates the payload of IP packets."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-mode-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0494","sequence":15540}],"title":"Mode of operation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Perfect Forward Secrecy","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using PFS reduces the impact of the compromise of a security association."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-perfe","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1000","sequence":15700}],"title":"Perfect Forward Secrecy","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protocol selection","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"IPsec contains two major protocols, the Authentication Header (AH) protocol and the Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP) protocol. To provide a secure Virtual Private Network style connection, authentication and encryption are needed. While the AH and ESP protocols can provide authentication, for the IP packet and the payload respectively, only the ESP protocol can provide encryption.\n\nAs the combined use of the AH protocol and the ESP protocol is not supported by Internet Key Exchange (IKE) version 2, the ESP protocol should be used for authentication and encryption of IPsec connections."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-proto","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0496","sequence":15560}],"title":"Protocol selection","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Pseudorandom function","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"IKE version 2 requires the use of a PRF to generate random data for cryptographic operations. The approved hashing algorithms that can be used for the PRF are HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA384 and HMAC-SHA512.\n\nFor interoperability and maintainability reasons, HMAC-SHA256 will not be approved beyond 2030."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-pseud","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1772","sequence":15620}],"title":"Pseudorandom function","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Security association lifetimes","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using a security association lifetime of less than four hours (14400 seconds) for IKE version 2 can provide a balance between security and usability."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-internet-protocol-security-secur","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0498","sequence":15680}],"title":"Security association lifetimes","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Using Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension\n\nWhen using IT equipment or applications that implement S/MIME, controls for using AACAs and AACPs in the ‘Cryptographic algorithms’ and ‘Cryptographic protocols’ sections of these guidelines will also need to be consulted."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-multipurpose-internet-mai","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-multipurpose-internet-mai","sequence":15520}],"title":"Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extension","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Secure Shell","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Using Secure Shell

When using IT equipment or applications that implement SSH, controls for using AACAs and AACPs in the ‘Cryptographic algorithms’ and ‘Cryptographic protocols’ sections of these guidelines will also need to be consulted.

# Further information

Further information on [configuring OpenSSH](#f832363c-d81e-4908-afd9-cf45047dd19c) is available from the OpenSSH project."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-configuring-secure-","sequence":15410},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-authentication-mech","sequence":15440},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-automated-remote-ac","sequence":15470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-ssh-agent","sequence":15490}],"title":"Secure Shell","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Authentication mechanisms","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As public key-based authentication schemes offer stronger authentication than password-based authentication schemes, due to being much less susceptible to brute-force attacks, they should be used for SSH connections. Furthermore, to protect SSH private keys, access to such keys should be protected via the use of passwords or key encryption keys."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-authentication-mech","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0485","sequence":15420},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1449","sequence":15430}],"title":"Authentication mechanisms","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Automated remote access","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"If using logins without a password for automated purposes, several security risks may arise, specifically:

- if access from unknown Internet Protocol (IP) addresses is not restricted, malicious actors could automatically authenticate to systems without needing to know any passwords
- if port forwarding is not disabled, or it is not configured securely, access may be gained to forwarded ports, creating a communication channel between malicious actors and a host
- if agent credential forwarding is enabled, malicious actors could connect to the stored authentication credentials and use them to connect to other trusted hosts, or even intranet hosts if port forwarding has been allowed as well
- if X11 forwarding is not disabled, malicious actors could gain control of displays as well as keyboard and mouse control functions
- if console access is allowed, every user who logs in to the console could run applications that are normally restricted to authenticated users.

To assist in mitigating these security risks, it is essential that the ‘forced command’ option is used to specify what command is executed and parameter checking is enabled."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-automated-remote-ac","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0487","sequence":15450},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0488","sequence":15460}],"title":"Automated remote access","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Configuring Secure Shell","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"SSH version 1 was found to have multiple vulnerabilities and was subsequently replaced by SSH version 2. As such, an organisation implementing SSH should disable the use of SSH version 1. In addition, several security risks exist when SSH is configured in an insecure manner. To mitigate these security risks, SSH should be configured as per the settings below.\n\nThe settings below are based on OpenSSH. An organisation using other implementations of SSH should adapt these settings to suit their SSH implementation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-configuring-secure-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1506","sequence":15390},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0484","sequence":15400}],"title":"Configuring Secure Shell","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"SSH-agent","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"SSH-agent and similar key caching applications manage private keys stored on workstations and servers. Specifically, when an SSH-agent launches, it requests a user’s password to unlock the user’s private key. Subsequent access to remote systems is then performed by the SSH-agent and does not require the user to re-enter their password. Screen locks and expiring key caches can be used to ensure that a user’s private key is not left unlocked for a long period."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-secure-shell-ssh-agent","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0489","sequence":15480}],"title":"SSH-agent","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Transport Layer Security","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Using Transport Layer Security

When using IT equipment or applications that implement TLS, controls for using AACAs and AACPs in the ‘Cryptographic algorithms’ and ‘Cryptographic protocols’ sections of these guidelines will also need to be consulted.

# Further information

Further information on implementing TLS can be found in ASD’s [Implementing certificates, TLS, HTTPS and opportunistic TLS](#b8cdbf18-31cb-4e10-bb43-e8488ee8c2f6) publication.

Further information on TLS filtering in gateways can be found in the ‘Web content filters’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-transport-layer-security","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-transport-layer-security-configu","sequence":15370}],"title":"Transport Layer Security","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Configuring Transport Layer Security","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The terms Secure Sockets Layer and TLS have traditionally been used interchangeably. However, Secure Sockets Layer and TLS version 1.2 and earlier are no longer considered suitable for use as an AACP. As such, an organisation implementing TLS should use only the latest version of TLS (i.e. TLS version 1.3). In addition, several security risks exist when TLS is configured in an insecure manner. To mitigate these security risks, TLS clients and servers should be configured to enforce secure settings at the time of the TLS handshake. In situations where this is not possible, such as for some multi-tenancy environments (e.g. content delivery networks), additional controls will need to be implemented. For example, by further restricting the permitted TLS configuration within Layer 7 authorisation logic."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cryptography-transport-layer-security-configu","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1139","sequence":15270},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1369","sequence":15280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1370","sequence":15290},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1372","sequence":15300},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1448","sequence":15310},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1373","sequence":15320},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1374","sequence":15330},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1375","sequence":15340},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1553","sequence":15350},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1453","sequence":15360}],"title":"Configuring Transport Layer Security","version":"2026.06.18"}
