Further information on data transfer processes and procedures can be found in the ‘Data transfers’ section of the [Guidelines for data transfers](#1eefdaf7-3991-4ba2-9439-79565f508c44)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-development-and-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-development-and-","sequence":2100},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-development-and-","sequence":2130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-development-and-","sequence":2150},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-development-and-","sequence":2170}],"title":"Development and maintenance of cyber security documentation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"System-specific cyber security documentation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to system-specific cyber security documentation

System-specific cyber security documentation, such as a system security plan, cyber security incident response plan, change and configuration management plan, continuous monitoring plan, security assessment report, and plan of action and milestones, supports the accurate and consistent application of policies, processes and procedures for systems. As such, it is important that they are developed by personnel with a good understanding of business requirements, technologies being used and cyber security matters.

System-specific cyber security documentation may be presented in multiple formats, including in wikis or other forms of document repositories. Furthermore, depending on the documentation framework used, details common to multiple systems could be consolidated into higher-level cyber security documentation.

# Further information

To assist with the development of system-specific cyber security documentation, a system security plan annex template, and an equivalent cloud controls matrix template, are available from the Australian Signals Directorate’s [Information security manual](#e7ce6e23-4bbb-45c1-a657-7e563c0837ed) webpage.

Further information on cyber security incident response activities can be found in the ‘Managing cyber security incidents’ and ‘Responding to cyber security incidents’ sections of the [Guidelines for cyber security incidents](#fe0138db-e83b-4a23-85d3-d84e1c22816f).

Further information on conducting security assessment activities as part of continuously monitoring the security of systems can be found in the ‘Security assessments’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2240},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2260},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-documentation-system-specific-","sequence":2300}],"title":"System-specific cyber security documentation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Managing cyber security incidents","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Cyber security events

A cyber security event is an occurrence of a system, service or network state indicating a possible breach of security policy, failure of safeguards or a previously unknown situation that may be relevant to security.

# Cyber security incidents

A cyber security incident is an unwanted or unexpected cyber security event, or a series of such events, that has either compromised business operations or has a significant probability of doing so.

# Cyber resilience

Cyber resilience is the ability to adapt to disruptions caused by cyber security incidents while maintaining continuous business operations. This includes the ability to detect, manage and recover from cyber security incidents.

# Further information

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c).

Further information on cyber security incident response plans can be found in the ‘System-specific cyber security documentation’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security documentation](#578d0434-6b3f-46f3-aad8-c7ac75c2ebcc).

Further information on preparing for and responding to cyber security incidents can be found in ASD’s [Cyber security incident response planning: Executive guidance](#403f72c6-3e85-4185-8df3-130b2a6b25b3) and [Cyber security incident response planning: Practitioner guidance](#041bce05-55ad-4a2a-93e3-c582d39fce94) publications.

Further information on understanding, identifying and preventing the insider threat can be found in the Attorney-General’s Department’s [Countering the Insider Threat: A guide for Australian Government](#fb60e251-ed4c-4781-96db-58a0225bca89) publication.

Further information on understanding, identifying and preventing the insider threat can also be found in the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation’s [Countering the insider threat](#fb60e251-ed4c-4781-96db-58a0225bca89) brochure and [Countering the insider threat: A security manager’s guide](#f6813648-fe24-4d45-9e45-c97b4021506e) publication.

Further information on understanding, identifying and preventing the insider threat can also be found on the United Kingdom’s National Protective Security Authority’s [Insider Risk Guidance](#c2a2f934-c5d6-46a7-86db-7fec1565058e) website.

Further information on developing, implementing and maintaining an insider threat mitigation program can be found in the United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Insider Threat Mitigation Guide](#c322926a-13b3-4efe-8573-06624418e8f5).

Further information on developing, implementing and maintaining an insider threat mitigation program can also be found in Carnegie Mellon University’s Software Engineering Institute’s [Common Sense Guide to Mitigating Insider Threats, Seventh Edition](#cad720b4-e47a-437d-b272-6958e738131d) publication.

Further information on reporting of cyber security incidents by service providers can be found in the ‘Managed services and cloud services’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on [reporting cybercrime incidents](#188466f6-be12-49ce-b99a-981e54b1663e) and [reporting cyber security incidents](#626d3582-3caf-49d6-89d5-4b8fdbbf1f31), including ASD’s [limited use obligation](#cae0ac6b-e69b-4359-bfd7-5ee5eff1049d), is available from ASD."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1250},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1310},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1330},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1350},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-incidents-managing-cyber-secur","sequence":1380}],"title":"Managing cyber security incidents","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Board of directors and executive committee","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on how the board of directors or executive committee can protect themselves from cyber threats can be found in the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) [Practical cyber security tips for business leaders](#2667c626-d3e8-4ee5-9044-2ada9f133df2) publication.

Further information on questions the board of directors or executive committee should be asking of their organisation can be found in ASD’s [Ten things to know about data security](#dc0469b3-3901-4d2c-8b22-7c6b6627ab97) publication.

Further information on how the board of directors or executive committee can plan for major cyber security incidents can be found in ASD’s [Planning for critical vulnerabilities: What the board of directors needs to know](#86814651-26b5-43f5-807d-0040c2d71169) publication.

Further information on cyber security considerations for the board of directors or executive committee during mergers, acquisitions and Machinery of Government changes can be found in ASD’s [Mergers, acquisitions and Machinery of Government changes](#fd4e2255-74ca-4b20-811c-a64a93282f57) publication.

Further information on cyber security responsibilities and duties of the board of directors or executive committee can be found in the United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Cyber Security Toolkit for Boards](#490f4622-8562-4333-a263-b64f660620e8)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","sequence":620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","sequence":640},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","sequence":680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","sequence":700},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-board-of-directors-and-e","sequence":720}],"title":"Board of directors and executive committee","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Chief information security officer","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Breadth of responsibilities

The role of the chief information security officer (CISO) within an organisation should extend to information technology (IT) and operational technology (OT). However, where appropriate and practical to do so, responsibility for OT cyber security may be delegated by the CISO.

Within this section, the breadth of responsibilities for IT and OT are collectively referenced under the banner of cyber security.

# Required skills and experience

The role of the CISO requires a combination of technical and soft skills, such as business acumen, leadership, communications and relationship building. Additionally, a CISO should adopt a continuous approach to learning and up-skilling to maintain pace with the cyber threat landscape and new technologies. It is expected that a CISO show innovation and imagination in conceiving and delivering cyber security strategies for their organisation.

# Further information

Further information on responding to cyber security incidents can be found in the ‘Managing cyber security incidents’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security incidents](#fe0138db-e83b-4a23-85d3-d84e1c22816f).

Further information on the development of a cyber security strategy can be found in the ‘Development and maintenance of cyber security documentation’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security documentation](#578d0434-6b3f-46f3-aad8-c7ac75c2ebcc).

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on the procurement of outsourced services can be found in the ‘Managed services and cloud services’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on cyber security awareness training programs can be found in the ‘Cyber security awareness training’ section of the [Guidelines for personnel security](#7d16ae67-87a7-4861-b939-e13ec279b5a2)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":750},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":800},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":830},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":910},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":930},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":970},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":1000},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-chief-information-securi","sequence":1020}],"title":"Chief information security officer","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"System owners","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on using the [Information security manual](#e7ce6e23-4bbb-45c1-a657-7e563c0837ed)’s six-step risk management framework can be found in the ‘Applying a risk-based approach to cyber security’ section of [Using the Information security manual](#8a965432-7e2e-4020-86e4-b38837f9be60).

Further information on [the purpose of IRAP](#84d3cbab-ab84-4981-a4f7-2bd469c8deaf) is available from ASD.

Further information on conducting security assessment activities as part of continuously monitoring the security of systems can be found in the ‘Security assessments’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-system-own","sequence":1060},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-protecting","sequence":1180},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-annual-rep","sequence":1200}],"title":"System owners","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Annual reporting of system security status","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Annual reporting by system owners on the security status of their systems to their authorising officer can assist the authorising officer in maintaining awareness of the security posture of systems within their organisation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-annual-rep","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1587","sequence":1190}],"title":"Annual reporting of system security status","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protecting systems and their resources","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Broadly, the risk management framework defined by the [Information security manual](#e7ce6e23-4bbb-45c1-a657-7e563c0837ed) has six steps: define the system, select controls, implement controls, assess controls, authorise the system and monitor the system. System owners are responsible for the implementation of this six-step risk management framework for each of their systems."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-protecting","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1633","sequence":1070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1203","sequence":1080},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1634","sequence":1090},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0009","sequence":1100},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1635","sequence":1110},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1636","sequence":1120},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1967","sequence":1130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0027","sequence":1140},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1968","sequence":1150},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1526","sequence":1160},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2021","sequence":1170}],"title":"Protecting systems and their resources","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"System ownership and oversight","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"System owners are responsible for ensuring the secure operation of their systems. However, system owners may delegate the day-to-day management and operation of their systems to system managers. It is recommended that system owners collaborate with their organisation’s internal cyber security teams or engage external cyber security specialists to assist them with their cyber security responsibilities."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-cyber-security-roles-system-owners-system-own","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1071","sequence":1040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1525","sequence":1050}],"title":"System ownership and oversight","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Data transfers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Performing data transfers

This section describes controls applicable to manual data transfers and data transfers using gateways or Cross Domain Solutions (CDSs). For data transfers using gateways or CDSs, the ‘Content filtering’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b) is also applicable.

# Further information

Further information on manual data transfers using removable media can be found in the ‘Media usage’ section of the [Guidelines for media](#b594c9c0-b42f-4f06-b643-38023275a5c7).

Further information on data transfers using gateways or CDSs can be found in the ‘Content filtering’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-data-transfer-p","sequence":16810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-user-responsibi","sequence":16830},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-manual-import-o","sequence":16860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-authorising-exp","sequence":16900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-manual-export-o","sequence":16940},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-monitoring-data","sequence":16980}],"title":"Data transfers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Authorising export of data","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Data exported from SECRET and TOP SECRET systems should be reviewed and authorised by a trustworthy source beforehand, such as the chief information security officer or one of their delegates. In doing so, all data authorised for export should be digitally signed by the trustworthy source."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-authorising-exp","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0664","sequence":16870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0675","sequence":16880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0665","sequence":16890}],"title":"Authorising export of data","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Data transfer processes and procedures","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Ensuring that data transfer processes and procedures are developed, implemented and maintained can facilitate consistent data transfers. In addition, to reduce the likelihood of Australian Eyes Only (AUSTEO), Australian Government Access Only (AGAO) and Releasable To (REL) data crossing into unsuitable foreign systems, it is important that additional processes and procedures are developed, implemented and maintained to prevent this from occurring. Depending on protective markings applied to REL data, it may be suitable for export to some foreign systems but not to others."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-data-transfer-p","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0663","sequence":16790},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1535","sequence":16800}],"title":"Data transfer processes and procedures","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Manual export of data","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When manually exporting data from systems, such as via the use of removable media, the data should be checked for unsuitable protective markings to reduce the likelihood of causing a data spill. In addition, data manually exported from SECRET and TOP SECRET systems will require additional assurances, for example, by validating digital signatures and checking for keywords within all textual data. Finally, in cases where security checks fail, data should be quarantined until it can be reviewed and subsequently approved or not approved for release."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-manual-export-o","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1187","sequence":16910},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0669","sequence":16920},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1779","sequence":16930}],"title":"Manual export of data","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Manual import of data","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When manually importing data to systems, such as via the use of removable media, the data should be scanned for malicious and active content to reduce the likelihood of causing a malicious code infection. In cases where security checks fail, data should be quarantined until it can be reviewed and subsequently approved or not approved for release."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-manual-import-o","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0657","sequence":16840},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1778","sequence":16850}],"title":"Manual import of data","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Monitoring data import and export","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"To ensure the ongoing confidentiality and integrity of systems, it is important to log all data transfers. This applies to all forms of data transfers, such as those performed using removable media, gateways or CDSs. Ideally, data transfer logs should contain information on who authorised the data transfer, what data was transferred, where the data was transferred from or to, when the data was transferred, why the data was transferred, and how the data was transferred. Monitoring of such activities, via periodic verification of data transfer logs, can assist in identifying abuse of data transfer privileges and any unusual usage patterns that may indicate attempts by malicious actors to surreptitiously import malicious code or exfiltrate data from SECRET and TOP SECRET systems."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-monitoring-data","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1586","sequence":16950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1294","sequence":16960},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0660","sequence":16970}],"title":"Monitoring data import and export","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"User responsibilities","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When users transfer data to or from systems, they should understand the potential consequences of their actions. This could include transferring data onto systems not authorised to handle the data, or the unintended introduction of malicious code to systems. As such, users should be held accountable for all data transfers that they perform."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-data-transfers-data-transfers-user-responsibi","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0661","sequence":16820}],"title":"User responsibilities","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Database servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on the functional separation of operating environments can be found in the ‘Virtualisation hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on encrypting communications can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1).

Further information on network segmentation and segregation can be found in the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on database management system applications can be found in the ‘Server application hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-functional-","sequence":12710},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-communicati","sequence":12730},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-network-env","sequence":12770},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-segregation","sequence":12790}],"title":"Database servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Communications between database servers and web servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Data communicated between database servers and web servers, especially over the internet, is susceptible to capture by malicious actors. As such, it is important that all data communicated between database servers and web servers is suitably encrypted using Australian Signals Directorate-approved cryptography."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-communicati","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1277","sequence":12720}],"title":"Communications between database servers and web servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Functional separation between database servers and web servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Due to the higher threat environment that web servers are typically exposed to, hosting database servers and web servers within the same operating environment increases the likelihood of database servers being compromised by malicious actors. This security risk can be mitigated by ensuring that database servers are functionally separated from web servers."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-functional-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1269","sequence":12700}],"title":"Functional separation between database servers and web servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Network environment","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Placing database servers on the same network segment as user workstations can increase the likelihood of database servers being compromised by malicious actors. Additionally, in cases where databases will only be accessed from their own database server, allowing remote access to the database server poses an unnecessary security risk."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-network-env","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1270","sequence":12740},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1271","sequence":12750},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1272","sequence":12760}],"title":"Network environment","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Segregation of development, testing, staging and production database servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using database servers across different environments, such as development, testing, staging and production environments, could result in accidental damage to their integrity or exposure of their hosted database contents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-database-servers-segregation","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1273","sequence":12780}],"title":"Segregation of development, testing, staging and production database servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Databases","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information\n\nFurther information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-database-register","sequence":12820},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-protecting-databas","sequence":12840},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-protecting-databas","sequence":12880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-segregation-of-dev","sequence":12900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-database-event-log","sequence":12920}],"title":"Databases","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Database event logging","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Centrally logging and analysing security-relevant events, including configuration changes, for databases can assist in monitoring the security posture of databases, detecting malicious behaviour and contributing to investigations following cyber security incidents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-database-event-log","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1537","sequence":12910}],"title":"Database event logging","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Database register","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Without knowledge of all the databases in an organisation, and their contents, an organisation will be unable to protect all of their assets. As such, it is important that a database register is developed, implemented, maintained and regularly verified."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-database-register","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1243","sequence":12810}],"title":"Database register","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protecting database contents","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Database administrators and database users should know the sensitivity or classification associated with databases and their contents. In cases where a database’s contents are all the same sensitivity or classification, an organisation should classify the entire database at this level and protect it as such. Alternatively, in cases where a database’s contents are of varying sensitivities or classifications, and database users have varying levels of access to the database’s contents, an organisation should protect the database’s contents at a more granular level.\n\nRestricting database users’ ability to access, insert, modify or remove database contents, based on their work duties, ensures that the likelihood of unauthorised access, modification or deletion of database contents is reduced. Furthermore, where concerns exist that the aggregation of separate pieces of content from within a database could lead to malicious actors determining more sensitive or classified content, the need-to-know principle can be enforced by using minimum privileges, database views and database roles. Alternatively, the content of concern could be separated by implementing multiple databases, each with restricted data sets."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-protecting-databas","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0393","sequence":12850},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1255","sequence":12860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1268","sequence":12870}],"title":"Protecting database contents","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Segregation of development, testing, staging and production databases","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using database contents from production environments in non-production environments, such as development, testing or staging environments, could result in inadequate protection being applied to the database contents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-database-systems-databases-segregation-of-dev","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1274","sequence":12890}],"title":"Segregation of development, testing, staging and production databases","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email gateways and servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on implementing opportunistic TLS encryption for email servers can be found in the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) [Implementing certificates, TLS, HTTPS and opportunistic TLS](#b8cdbf18-31cb-4e10-bb43-e8488ee8c2f6) publication.

Further information on implementing SPF, DKIM and DMARC can be found in ASD’s [How to combat fake emails](#d3572f80-d9d6-4023-b6e6-23720bb0d618) publication.

Further information on engaging the services of email service providers for marketing or filtering purposes can be found in ASD’s [Marketing and filtering email service providers](#e4eee462-f6e5-4921-a8fe-26f3de6c6f45) publication.

Further information on email content filtering can be found in the ‘Content filtering’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on email content filtering can be found in ASD’s [Malicious email mitigation strategies](#6136b137-0cc9-4d21-94ee-0abfac2b4e18) publication.

Further information on email security can be found in the following National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) publications:

- NIST Special Publication (SP) 800-45 Version 2, [Guidelines on Electronic Mail Security](#b383a647-7627-4450-99b6-640ae554c2f1)
- NIST SP 800-177 Rev. 1, [Trustworthy Email](#4b0f2fa7-f9d6-48d8-b14c-527939808882)
- NIST SP 1800-6, [Domain Name System-Based Electronic Mail Security](#55038d94-7587-4234-a18c-fb459fcdb295)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-centralised-","sequence":13130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-gatewa","sequence":13150},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-open-relay-e","sequence":13170},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-server","sequence":13200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-sender-polic","sequence":13240},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-domainkeys-i","sequence":13280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-domain-based","sequence":13310},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-conten","sequence":13330},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-blocking-sus","sequence":13350},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-notification","sequence":13370}],"title":"Email gateways and servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Blocking suspicious emails","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Blocking specific types of suspicious emails, such as where the email source address uses an internal domain, or internal subdomain, reduces the likelihood of phishing emails entering an organisation’s network."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-blocking-sus","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1502","sequence":13340}],"title":"Blocking suspicious emails","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Centralised email gateways","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When routing emails via centralised email gateways, it will be easier for an organisation to deploy Sender Policy Framework (SPF), DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM), Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance (DMARC) and protective marking checks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-centralised-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0569","sequence":13110},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0571","sequence":13120}],"title":"Centralised email gateways","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"DMARC enables a domain owner to specify what action receiving email servers should take as a result of domain alignment, SPF and DKIM checks. For emails that do not pass DMARC checks, this includes ‘reject’ (emails are rejected), ‘quarantine’ (emails are marked as spam) or ‘none’ (no action is taken).\n\nDMARC also provides a reporting feature that enables a domain owner to receive reports on the actions taken by receiving email servers. While this feature does not mitigate malicious emails sent to the domain owner’s organisation, it can give the domain owner some visibility of attempts by malicious actors to spoof their organisation’s domains."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-domain-based","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1540","sequence":13290},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1799","sequence":13300}],"title":"Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"DomainKeys Identified Mail","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"DKIM enables the detection of spoofed email contents. This is achieved by DKIM records specifying the public key used to verify the digital signature in an email. Specifically, if the signed digest in an email header does not match the signed contents of the email, verification will not pass."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-domainkeys-i","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0861","sequence":13250},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1026","sequence":13260},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1027","sequence":13270}],"title":"DomainKeys Identified Mail","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email content filtering","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content filtering performed on email bodies and attachments provides a defence-in-depth approach to preventing malicious code being introduced into networks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-conten","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1234","sequence":13320}],"title":"Email content filtering","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email gateway maintenance activities","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As backup and alternative email gateways are often poorly maintained in terms of patches and email content filtering, malicious actors will often seek to exploit this when sending malicious emails to an organisation. As such, it is important that backup and alternative email gateways are maintained at the same standard as an organisation’s primary email gateway."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-gatewa","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0570","sequence":13140}],"title":"Email gateway maintenance activities","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email server transport encryption","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Emails can be intercepted anywhere between originating email servers and destination email servers. Implementing opportunistic Transport Layer Security (TLS) encryption can mitigate this security risk. However, opportunistic TLS encryption is susceptible to downgrade attacks. To mitigate this security risk, Mail Transfer Agent Strict Transport Security (MTA-STS) allows domain owners to indicate that email transfers should only occur if satisfactory TLS encryption is negotiated beforehand.\n\nIn support of MTA-STS implementations, TLS Reporting provides a mechanism for a domain owner to publish a location where reports can be submitted regarding the success or failure of attempts to initiate encrypted connections when sending emails to a specified domain."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-email-server","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0572","sequence":13180},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1589","sequence":13190}],"title":"Email server transport encryption","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Notifications of undeliverable emails","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Notifications of undeliverable emails are commonly sent by receiving email servers when emails cannot be delivered, usually because destination addresses are invalid. Due to the common spamming practice of spoofing sender addresses, this often results in many undeliverable email notifications being sent to innocent third parties. Sending notifications of undeliverable emails only to senders that can be verified via SPF, or other trusted means, avoids contributing to this problem while allowing legitimate senders to be notified."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-notification","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1024","sequence":13360}],"title":"Notifications of undeliverable emails","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Open relay email servers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"An open relay email server (or open mail relay) is an email server that is configured to allow anyone on the internet to send emails through it. Such configurations are highly undesirable as spammers and worms can exploit them."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-open-relay-e","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0567","sequence":13160}],"title":"Open relay email servers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Sender Policy Framework","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"SPF aids in the detection of spoofed emails by specifying a list of hosts or Internet Protocol (IP) addresses that are allowed to send emails on behalf of a specified domain or subdomain. If an email server is not in the SPF record for a domain or subdomain, SPF verification will not pass. In specifying SPF records, domain owners should ensure that they delegate the minimum necessary set of hosts or IP addresses necessary for sending emails. In addition, extra care should be taken when delegating to hosts or IP addresses not under an organisation’s control."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-gateways-and-servers-sender-polic","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0574","sequence":13210},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1183","sequence":13220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1151","sequence":13230}],"title":"Sender Policy Framework","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email usage","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information\n\nFurther information on protective markings for emails, where applicable, can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a) and its associated [Australian Government Email Protective Marking Standard](#11372646-3106-42c6-b3e1-444b3f630462)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-email-usage-policy","sequence":12960},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-webmail-services","sequence":12980},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-protective-markings-for-ema","sequence":13000},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-protective-marking-tools","sequence":13040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-handling-emails-with-inappr","sequence":13070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-email-distribution-lists","sequence":13090}],"title":"Email usage","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email distribution lists","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"In some cases, the membership and nationality of members of email distribution lists will be unknown. As such, emails containing Australian Eyes Only, Australian Government Access Only or Releasable To data that are sent to email distribution lists could accidentally cause a data spill."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-email-distribution-lists","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0269","sequence":13080}],"title":"Email distribution lists","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Email usage policy","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As there are many security risks associated with the use of email services, it is important that an organisation develops, implements and maintains an email usage policy governing its use."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-email-usage-policy","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0264","sequence":12950}],"title":"Email usage policy","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Handling emails with inappropriate, invalid or missing protective markings","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"It is important that email servers are configured to block emails with inappropriate protective markings. For example, blocking inbound and outbound emails with protective markings higher than the sensitivity or classification of the receiving system, as this will help prevent a data spill from occurring. In doing so, it is important to inform the intended recipients of blocked inbound emails, and the senders of blocked outbound emails, that this has occurred.\n\nIf emails are received with invalid or missing protective markings, they may still be passed to their intended recipients. However, the recipients will have an obligation to determine appropriate protective markings if emails are to be responded to, forwarded or printed. If unsure, original senders of emails should be contacted to provide guidance on appropriate protective markings."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-handling-emails-with-inappr","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0565","sequence":13050},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1023","sequence":13060}],"title":"Handling emails with inappropriate, invalid or missing protective markings","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protective marking tools","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Requiring user involvement in the protective marking of emails ensures a conscious decision is made by users. This lessens the chance of incorrect protective markings being applied to emails. In addition, allowing users to select only protective markings for which a system is authorised to process, store or communicate lessens the chance of users inadvertently over-classifying emails.\n\nEmail content filters may only check the most recent protective marking applied to emails. Therefore, when users are responding to or forwarding emails, requiring protective markings that are at least as high as that of emails that are received will help email content filters prevent emails being sent to systems that are not authorised to handle their original sensitivity or classification."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-protective-marking-tools","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0271","sequence":13010},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0272","sequence":13020},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1089","sequence":13030}],"title":"Protective marking tools","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protective markings for emails","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Implementing protective markings for emails helps to prevent data spills, such as unauthorised data being released into the public domain. In doing so, it is important that protective markings reflect the highest sensitivity or classification of the subject, body and attachments of emails."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-protective-markings-for-ema","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0270","sequence":12990}],"title":"Protective markings for emails","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Webmail services","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When users access non-approved webmail services, they often bypass controls that have been implemented by an organisation, such as email content filtering. To mitigate this security risk, access to non-approved webmail services should be blocked."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-email-email-usage-webmail-services","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0267","sequence":12970}],"title":"Webmail services","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Enterprise mobility","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to enterprise mobility

Enterprise mobility generally refers to situations in which personnel work in a mobile manner, such as part of office hot-desking arrangements, when working from home, when travelling or simply when outside the office environment during normal business hours. While enterprise mobility has traditionally been used to refer to the use of mobile devices, such as smartphones, tablets and laptop computers, it is increasingly being applied to the use of desktop computers as part of working from home arrangements.

This section applies to privately owned and organisation-owned mobile devices and desktop computers that use either a mobile operating system or a desktop operating system.

# Further information

Further information on allowing the use of privately owned mobile devices and desktop computers by personnel to access their organisation’s systems or data can be found in the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) [Bring Your Own Device for executives](#0e982561-93dc-4cf7-9a98-e91411e99ca6) publication.

Further information and specific guidance on enterprise mobility can be found in ASD’s [Risk management of enterprise mobility (including Bring Your Own Device)](#e8ec7a1f-775d-4b79-a164-0752278177fa) publication.

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on the procurement and use of online services can be found in the ‘Managed services and cloud services’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-priva","sequence":4980},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-organ","sequence":5000},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-mobil","sequence":5030}],"title":"Enterprise mobility","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Mobile devices and desktop computers accessing the internet","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When mobile devices and desktop computers access the internet, doing so via an organisation’s internet gateway rather than directly can enable the provision of additional security functionality, such as web content filtering and protective Domain Name System services. However, in some cases, an organisation may instead accept the security risks associated with allowing direct connections to specific online services, such as web conferencing services and collaboration tools, for performance reasons.\n\nWhen connecting mobile devices and desktop computers to an organisation’s internet gateway via a Virtual Private Network (VPN) connection, a split tunnel VPN can enable indirect access into the organisation’s network from other networks, such as the internet. As such, if split tunnelling is not disabled, there is an increased security risk that the VPN connection could be used as an intrusion vector by malicious actors."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-mobil","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0874","sequence":5010},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0705","sequence":5020}],"title":"Mobile devices and desktop computers accessing the internet","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Organisation-owned mobile devices and desktop computers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"If an organisation chooses to issue personnel with organisation-owned mobile devices or desktop computers to access their organisation’s classified systems or data, they should ensure that it does not present an unacceptable security risk. This can be achieved in part by enforcing the separation of classified data and personal data."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-organ","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1482","sequence":4990}],"title":"Organisation-owned mobile devices and desktop computers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Privately owned mobile devices and desktop computers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Allowing privately owned mobile devices and desktop computers to access an organisation’s systems or data can increase liability risk. As such, an organisation should seek legal advice to ascertain whether this scenario affects compliance with relevant legislation, such as the [Privacy Act 1988](#6fac5a84-b86e-405f-b2b3-8c13ecee4a02) and the [Archives Act 1983](#e0d3b5ea-6a5a-400c-8daa-8a8059816a06). Furthermore, if an organisation chooses to allow personnel to use privately owned mobile devices or desktop computers to access their organisation’s classified systems or data, they should ensure that it does not present an unacceptable security risk. This can be achieved in part through the enforced separation of classified data and personal data, preventing the storage of classified data on privately owned mobile devices and desktop computers, and disallowing the use of unapproved artificial intelligence agents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-enterprise-mobility-priva","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1297","sequence":4930},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1400","sequence":4940},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1866","sequence":4950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2095","sequence":4960},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0694","sequence":4970}],"title":"Privately owned mobile devices and desktop computers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Mobile device management","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to mobile device management

This section describes the management of privately owned and organisation-owned mobile devices, such as smartphones and tablets that use a mobile operating system. Alternatively, guidance for privately owned and organisation-owned mobile devices that use a desktop operating system is available in the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44) and the [Guidelines for system management](#c6ca6620-ccd5-4c5d-b97c-9d92f1162948).

# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on evaluated products can be found in the ‘Evaluated product procurement’ section of the [Guidelines for evaluated products](#a699a3aa-828d-479b-b50b-98127bb19437).

Further information on Common Criteria Protection Profiles for mobile devices can be found in the following United States’ National Information Assurance Partnership publications:

- [Protection Profile for Mobile Device Management Version 4.0](#ed6aa6ab-545d-48eb-92bc-9ad763d4b868)
- [Protection Profile for Mobile Device Fundamentals Version 3.3](#f160b04e-1a32-4dd5-816e-1cba8c97982c).

Further information on hardening mobile platforms can be found in the following ASD publications:

- [Security configuration guide: Apple iOS 14 devices](#d2910e61-da88-4d29-b860-83c25161fd18)
- [Security configuration guide: Samsung Galaxy S10, S20 and Note 20 devices](#616fce77-495b-472a-a552-7593ab64f188)
- [Security configuration guide: Viasat Mobile Dynamic Defense](#daae37ef-262f-4b20-bfb7-a27901f3c2db).

Further information on encrypting mobile devices and their communications can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","sequence":5070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","sequence":5100},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","sequence":5130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","sequence":5160},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","sequence":5260}],"title":"Mobile device management","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Maintaining mobile device security","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Poorly secured mobile devices are more vulnerable to compromise and can provide malicious actors with a potential access point into any connected systems. Although an organisation may initially provide secure mobile devices, their security posture may degrade over time if personnel can install non-approved applications and disable or modify security functionality. Furthermore, it is important that security updates are applied to mobile devices as soon as they become available to maintain their security posture."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-management-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1886","sequence":5170},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2096","sequence":5180},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2097","sequence":5190},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1887","sequence":5200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1888","sequence":5210},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2098","sequence":5220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0863","sequence":5230},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0864","sequence":5240},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1366","sequence":5250}],"title":"Maintaining mobile device security","version":"2026.06.18"}
