props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Mobile device usage","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to mobile device usage

This section describes the usage of privately owned and organisation-owned mobile devices that use either a mobile operating system or a desktop operating system.

# Further information

Further information on Bluetooth security can be found in National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-121 Rev. 2, [Guide to Bluetooth Security](#315744ea-3523-4c93-838d-fe2848ac4181).

Further information on usage of mobile devices in SECRET and TOP SECRET areas can be found in the ‘Facilities and systems’ section of the [Guidelines for physical security](#4deea7ea-18a2-4fd7-8b25-0b3603215df2).

Further information on security briefcases can be found in the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation’s Security Equipment Guide-005, Briefcases for the Carriage of Security Classified Information. This publication is available from the Protective Security Policy GovTEAMS community or the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation by email.

Further information on approved multi-use satchels, pouches and transit bags can be found on the Security Construction and Equipment Committee’s [Security Equipment Evaluated Products List](#f7e13f7b-eb48-4294-bd01-9c22c756d96b)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-mobil","sequence":5290},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-perso","sequence":5320},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-using","sequence":5340},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-using","sequence":5400},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-conne","sequence":5420},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-using","sequence":5450},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-using","sequence":5490},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-maint","sequence":5530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-mobil","sequence":5560},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-befor","sequence":5600},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-while","sequence":5620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-after","sequence":5650}],"title":"Mobile device usage","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"After travelling overseas with mobile devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Following overseas travel with mobile devices, personnel should take appropriate precautions to ensure that they do not pose an undue security risk to their organisation’s systems. In most cases, sanitising and resetting mobile devices, including all removable media, will be sufficient. However, upon returning from high or extreme risk countries, additional precautions will likely be needed."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-after","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1300","sequence":5630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1556","sequence":5640}],"title":"After travelling overseas with mobile devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Before travelling overseas with mobile devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Personnel travelling overseas with mobile devices face additional security risks compared to travelling domestically, especially when travelling to high or extreme risk countries. As such, appropriate precautions should be taken. Personnel should also be aware that when they leave Australian borders, they also leave behind any expectations of privacy."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-befor","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1298","sequence":5570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1554","sequence":5580},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1555","sequence":5590}],"title":"Before travelling overseas with mobile devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Connecting mobile devices to connected vehicles","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Connected vehicles often monitor, record, aggregate and communicate data on vehicle users, their connected mobile devices and their surrounding environment. In many cases, this data is then sent to the vehicle manufacturer, which could then be sold to or shared with third parties."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-conne","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2099","sequence":5410}],"title":"Connecting mobile devices to connected vehicles","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Maintaining control of mobile devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As mobile devices are portable in nature, and can be easily lost or stolen, personnel should maintain continual direct supervision of them when being actively used and ensure they are carried or stored in a secured state when not being actively used. While mobile devices may be encrypted, the effectiveness of encryption might be reduced if they are lost or stolen while in sleep mode or powered on with a locked screen."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-maint","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0871","sequence":5500},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0870","sequence":5510},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1084","sequence":5520}],"title":"Maintaining control of mobile devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Mobile device emergency sanitisation processes and procedures","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The sanitisation of mobile devices in emergencies can assist in reducing the potential for compromise of data by malicious actors. This may be achieved by using a remote wipe capability, a cryptographic key zeroise or sanitisation function if present."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-mobil","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0701","sequence":5540},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0702","sequence":5550}],"title":"Mobile device emergency sanitisation processes and procedures","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Personnel awareness","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As mobile devices often have voice and data communication capabilities, personnel should be made aware of the sensitivity or classification of voice and data that mobile devices have been approved to process, store and communicate. In addition, personnel should be made aware of common security practices for mobile device usage."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-perso","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1083","sequence":5300},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1299","sequence":5310}],"title":"Personnel awareness","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using mobile devices in public spaces","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Personnel should be aware of the environment in which they use mobile devices to view or communicate sensitive or classified data. Including by ensuring that sensitive or classified data is not observed by other parties in public areas, such as on public transport, in transit lounges and at coffee shops. In some cases, privacy filters can be applied to the screen of a mobile device to prevent onlookers from reading content off its screen.\n\nIn addition, personnel should maintain awareness of the environments from which they conduct sensitive or classified phone calls and the potential for their conversations to be overheard."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-using","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0866","sequence":5460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1145","sequence":5470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1644","sequence":5480}],"title":"Using mobile devices in public spaces","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"While travelling overseas with mobile devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Personnel lose control of mobile devices and removable media any time they are not on their person. In addition, allowing untrusted people to access mobile devices provides an opportunity for them to be tampered with."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-enterprise-mobility-mobile-device-usage-while","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1088","sequence":5610}],"title":"While travelling overseas with mobile devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Evaluated product procurement","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# High Assurance evaluations

An evaluated product provides a level of assurance in its security functionality that an unevaluated product does not. To assist in providing this assurance, the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) performs evaluations for products used to protect SECRET and TOP SECRET data via its High Assurance Evaluation Program.

# Common Criteria evaluations

The Australian Certification Authority within ASD certifies product evaluations conducted by licensed commercial facilities, in accordance with the Common Criteria (i.e. the International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 15408 series), as part of the Australian Information Security Evaluation Program (AISEP).

For an organisation seeking to procure evaluated products, the Common Criteria’s [Certified Products List](#29b04eaf-5f57-4ea1-a453-8047826dccf5) contains a list of products that have been evaluated, certified and mutually recognised in accordance with the Common Criteria and the Common Criteria Recognition Arrangement (CCRA).

# Cryptographic evaluations

Some CCRA schemes leverage the [Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Program](#42e7d99d-9709-4355-a54d-98959c552aa1) for the evaluation of cryptographic algorithms used by cryptographic modules within evaluated products. In such cases, cryptographic evaluations are performed by Cryptographic and Security Testing laboratories that are accredited by the United States’ National Voluntary Laboratory Accreditation Program to International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 17025:2017, [General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories](#edd67903-94ef-4edd-9789-73e0141cd77c).

# Protection Profiles

A Protection Profile (PP) is a technology-specific document that defines the security functionality that must be included in a Common Criteria evaluated product to mitigate specific cyber threats. PPs can be published by a recognised CCRA scheme or by the CCRA body itself. PPs published by the CCRA body are referred to as collaborative PPs.

ASD recognises all collaborative PPs listed on the Common Criteria website, and will consider national PPs listed on the United States’ National Information Assurance Partnership website, in addition to those listed on ASD’s AISEP webpage. Where a PP does not exist, an evaluation based on an Evaluation Assurance Level (EAL) may be accepted. Such evaluations are capped at EAL2+ as this represents the best balance between completion time and meaningful security assurance gains.

# Evaluation documentation

An organisation choosing to use Common Criteria evaluated products can determine their suitability by reviewing their evaluation documentation. This includes the security target and certification report.

Products that are undergoing a Common Criteria evaluation will not have published evaluation documentation. However, documentation can be obtained from ASD if a product is being evaluated through the AISEP. For a product that is in evaluation through a foreign scheme, the product’s vendor can be contacted directly for further information.

# Further information

Further information on the [High Assurance Evaluation Program](#e4ef23da-2683-4356-93c4-a2412c9d52ee) is available from ASD.

Further information on the [AISEP](#57c58870-2ebb-4fce-b995-0c86cb64002f) is available from ASD.

Further information on Common Criteria evaluated products can be found on the Common Criteria’s [Certified Products List](#29b04eaf-5f57-4ea1-a453-8047826dccf5).

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-procurem","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-procurem","sequence":5690},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-procurem","sequence":5720}],"title":"Evaluated product procurement","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Evaluated product usage","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Evaluated configuration

An evaluated product is operating in an evaluated configuration if:

- functionality that it uses was in the scope of the evaluation and it is implemented in the specified manner
- only product updates that have been assessed through maintenance and re-evaluation activities (known as assurance continuity) have been applied
- the environment complies with assumptions or organisational security policies stated in the evaluation documentation.

# Unevaluated configuration

An evaluated product is operating in an unevaluated configuration when it does not meet the requirements of the evaluated configuration and guidance provided in its certification report.

# Patching evaluated products

In most cases, the latest patched version of an evaluated product will be more secure than an older unpatched version. While the application of patches will not normally place an evaluated product into an unevaluated configuration, some vendors may include new functionality that has not been evaluated with their patches. In such cases, an organisation should use their judgement to determine whether this deviation from the evaluated configuration constitutes additional security risk or not.

# Further information

Further information on patching or updating IT equipment can be found in the ‘System maintenance’ section of the [Guidelines for system management](#c6ca6620-ccd5-4c5d-b97c-9d92f1162948).

Further information on the installation, configuration, administration and operation of Common Criteria products is available from vendors and can be found in evaluation documentation on the Common Criteria’s [Certified Products List](#29b04eaf-5f57-4ea1-a453-8047826dccf5).

Further information on the installation, configuration, administration and operation of high assurance IT equipment is available from ASD."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-usage","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-usage-us","sequence":5760}],"title":"Evaluated product usage","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using evaluated products","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Product evaluation provides assurance that a product and its security functionality will operate as expected when used in a clearly defined configuration. The scope of the evaluation specifies how the product is to be installed, configured, administered and operated, as well as the security functionality to be used. Using an evaluated product in an unevaluated configuration could introduce security risks that were not considered as part of the product’s evaluation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-evaluated-products-evaluated-product-usage-us","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0289","sequence":5740},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0290","sequence":5750}],"title":"Using evaluated products","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Content filtering","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Content filtering techniques

The following content filtering techniques should be considered as part of an organisation’s content filtering implementation for gateways and CDSs:

- **Antivirus scans:** Scans files for viruses and other malicious code.
- **Automated dynamic analysis:** Analyses executable files run in a sandbox to detect suspicious behaviour.
- **File extension checks:** Checks file extensions to determine purported file types.
- **File format checks:** Checks files conform to defined file format specifications.
- **File type checks:** Checks file headers to determine actual file types.
- **Keyword checks:** Checks files for keywords that could indicate undesirable content.
- **Metadata checks:** Checks files for metadata that should be removed.
- **Protective marking checks:** Checks files for protective markings that may indicate undesirable content.
- **Manual inspections:** Involves the manual inspection of files for suspicious or undesirable content that an automated system may miss, which is particularly important for multimedia and content rich files.

# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on performing data transfers can be found in the ‘Data transfers’ section of the [Guidelines for data transfers](#1eefdaf7-3991-4ba2-9439-79565f508c44)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-performing-content","sequence":16500},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-encrypted-files","sequence":16520},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-archive-files","sequence":16550},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-antivirus-scanning","sequence":16570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-automated-dynamic-","sequence":16590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-allowing-specific-","sequence":16610},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-validation","sequence":16630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-checking","sequence":16650},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-conversion","sequence":16670},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-sanitisati","sequence":16690},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-validating-file-in","sequence":16710}],"title":"Content filtering","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Allowing specific content types","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Creating and enforcing an organisation-approved list of allowed file types can reduce the attack surface of networks. For example, a content filter in an email gateway might only allow Microsoft Office files and Portable Document Format (PDF) files."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-allowing-specific-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0649","sequence":16600}],"title":"Allowing specific content types","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Antivirus scanning","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Antivirus scanning can be used to detect malicious files. In doing so, multiple different scanning engines should be used to increase the likelihood of identifying any malicious files."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-antivirus-scanning","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1288","sequence":16560}],"title":"Antivirus scanning","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Archive files","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Archive files can be used to bypass content filtering checks if content filters do not handle such files correctly. Ensuring content filters recognise archive files will ensure the embedded files they contain are subject to the same content filtering checks as un-archived files.\n\nArchive files can be constructed in a manner that can result in a denial of service to content filters due to processor, memory or disk space exhaustion. To limit the likelihood of such situations, content filters can specify resource constraints while unpacking archive files. If these constraints are exceeded, content filtering checks should be terminated."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-archive-files","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1289","sequence":16530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1290","sequence":16540}],"title":"Archive files","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Automated dynamic analysis","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Analysing executable files in a sandbox can be an effective method to detect suspicious behaviour upon file execution, such as network traffic, creation or modification of files, or system configuration changes."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-automated-dynamic-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1389","sequence":16580}],"title":"Automated dynamic analysis","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Content checking","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content checking, such as keyword checks, metadata checks and protective marking checks, aims to ensure that files do not contain any content that could cause a data spill or facilitate unauthorised export of data from systems."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-checking","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1965","sequence":16640}],"title":"Content checking","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Content conversion","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content conversion can be an effective method to render malicious code harmless by converting one file type to another file type. However, some file types will not benefit from content conversion. Examples of content conversion include:

- converting Microsoft Word documents to PDF files
- converting Microsoft PowerPoint presentations to image files
- converting Microsoft Excel spreadsheets to comma-separated values files
- converting PDF documents to plain text files."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-conversion","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1286","sequence":16660}],"title":"Content conversion","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Content sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content sanitisation is the process of rendering files safe by removing or altering active content while leaving the original content as intact as possible, such as by removing macros from Microsoft Office files or removing JavaScript sections from PDF files."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-sanitisati","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1287","sequence":16680}],"title":"Content sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Content validation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content validation, such as file format checks, aims to ensure that files conform to defined file format specifications. In performing content validation, any malformed content may indicate the presence of unauthorised content or malicious code, such as that designed to exploit known vulnerabilities in operating systems and applications."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-content-validation","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1284","sequence":16620}],"title":"Content validation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Encrypted files","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As encryption can be used to bypass content filtering checks, this poses a security risk in that malicious code could enter networks, or data could be exfiltrated from networks, undetected. In addition, encrypted files could mask data at a higher classification than that authorised to pass through gateways or CDSs, which could result in a data spill. As such, encrypted files should be decrypted to undergo content filtering checks.\n\nWhere a requirement to preserve the confidentiality of encrypted files exists, an organisation may also consider a dedicated system to enable encrypted files to be decrypted in an appropriately secure environment before being subjected to all applicable content filtering checks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-encrypted-files","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1293","sequence":16510}],"title":"Encrypted files","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Performing content filtering","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Content filters perform an important function within gateways and CDSs by reducing the likelihood of unauthorised content or malicious code from entering or exiting networks. In performing content filtering checks, some content will be readily identifiable as malicious, or cannot be inspected, while other content, such as active content, may be deemed suspicious depending on what is considered normal behaviour for content passing through gateways and CDSs within an organisation. Finally, when content filters are used by CDSs, their assurance requirements necessitate rigorous security testing to ensure they perform as expected and cannot be bypassed."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-performing-content","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0659","sequence":16460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0651","sequence":16470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0652","sequence":16480},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1524","sequence":16490}],"title":"Performing content filtering","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Validating file integrity","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"If files passing through gateways or CDSs contain a form of integrity protection, such as a digital signature or cryptographic checksum, content filters should validate their integrity. In doing so, the failure of any integrity checks may indicate that files have been tampered with."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-content-filtering-validating-file-in","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0677","sequence":16700}],"title":"Validating file integrity","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cross Domain Solutions","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to Cross Domain Solutions

A Cross Domain Solution (CDS) is a system comprised of security-enforcing functions tailored to mitigate specific security risks associated with accessing or transferring data between different security domains. CDSs may be an integrated appliance or, more commonly, be composed of discrete technologies or sub-systems, with each sub-system consisting of hardware or software components.

This section describes the controls applicable to CDSs and extends upon the ‘Gateways’ section of these guidelines. Additional sections of these guidelines should also be consulted depending on the types of CDSs being deployed.

Personnel involved in the planning, design, implementation or assessment of CDSs should also refer to ASD’s [Introduction to Cross Domain Solutions](#86847be4-3a06-4d2f-9952-447158593e0d) and [Fundamentals of Cross Domain Solutions](#f32020e9-4cb3-486b-bcbb-6fb6719f58a5) publications.

# Types of Cross Domain Solutions

This section defines two types of CDSs, Transfer CDSs and Access CDSs. These definitions are closely aligned with how CDSs are described and sold by vendors. However, vendors may also offer combined Access and Transfer CDSs.

In defining the functionality of different types of CDSs, Transfer CDSs facilitate the transfer of data in one direction (unidirectional) or multiple directions (bi-directional) between different security domains. In comparison, Access CDSs provide users with access to multiple security domains from a single device. However, while Access CDSs allow interaction with different security domains, they do not allow users to move data between the different security domains.

# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on evaluated products can be found in the ‘Evaluated product procurement’ section of the [Guidelines for evaluated products](#a699a3aa-828d-479b-b50b-98127bb19437).

Further information on designing, configuring and managing networks can be found in the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c).

Further information on event logging for network devices in Cross Domain Solutions can also be found in ASD’s [Priority logs for SIEM ingestion: Practitioner guidance](#1dbda98a-4e8b-4a52-b4f7-9d1a895fd324) publication.

Further information on cyber security awareness training can be found in the ‘Cyber security awareness training’ section of the [Guidelines for personnel security](#7d16ae67-87a7-4861-b939-e13ec279b5a2)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-implementing-","sequence":16040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-consultation-","sequence":16060},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-separation-of","sequence":16100},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-cross-domain-","sequence":16130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-user-training","sequence":16150}],"title":"Cross Domain Solutions","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Consultation on Cross Domain Solutions","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As CDSs can be complex to implement and manage securely, it is critical that when an organisation is planning, designing, implementing or introducing additional connectivity to CDSs that ASD is consulted and any directions provided by ASD are complied with."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-consultation-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0597","sequence":16050}],"title":"Consultation on Cross Domain Solutions","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cross Domain Solution event logging","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"CDSs should have comprehensive event logging capabilities to ensure accountability of users for all activities they undertake. Furthermore, effective security monitoring practices can increase the likelihood that operational failures will be detected.\n\nIn addition, centrally logging and analysing security-relevant events, including configuration changes, for CDSs can assist in monitoring the security posture of CDSs, detecting malicious behaviour and contributing to investigations following cyber security incidents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-cross-domain-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0670","sequence":16110},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1523","sequence":16120}],"title":"Cross Domain Solution event logging","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Implementing Cross Domain Solutions","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As there are significant security risks associated with connecting SECRET or TOP SECRET networks to other networks in different security domains, CDSs will need to be implemented."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-implementing-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0626","sequence":16030}],"title":"Implementing Cross Domain Solutions","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Separation of data flows","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"To ensure that data flows are appropriately controlled within CDSs, it is important that isolated upward and downward network paths are implemented. This, in turn, should be supported by independent security-enforcing functions and protocol breaks at each network layer."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-separation-of","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0635","sequence":16070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1522","sequence":16080},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1521","sequence":16090}],"title":"Separation of data flows","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"User training","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"To assist in preventing cyber security incidents, it is important that users know how to use CDSs securely. This can be achieved by training users on the secure use of CDSs before access is granted."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-cross-domain-solutions-user-training","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0610","sequence":16140}],"title":"User training","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using diodes","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Diodes enforce one-way data flows, making it more difficult for malicious actors to use the same network path to launch an intrusion and exfiltrate data afterwards. As such, diodes should be used for controlling the data flow of unidirectional gateways."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-diodes-using-diodes","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0643","sequence":16240},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0645","sequence":16250},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1157","sequence":16260},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1158","sequence":16270}],"title":"Using diodes","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using firewalls","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When implementing gateways between an organisation’s networks and public network infrastructure, an organisation should implement firewalls to protect themselves from intrusions that may originate from the public network infrastructure. In addition, when an organisation’s networks connect to another organisation’s networks, both organisations should implement independent firewalls to protect themselves from intrusions that may originate from each other’s networks. This requirement may not be necessary in cases where shared network infrastructure is used only as a transport medium and encryption is applied to all network traffic."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-firewalls-using-firewalls","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1528","sequence":16170},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0639","sequence":16180}],"title":"Using firewalls","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Gateways","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to gateways

Gateways securely manage data flows between connected networks from different security domains. In doing so, gateways take on the highest sensitivity or classification of connected security domains.

This section describes controls applicable to all types of gateways. Additional sections of these guidelines should also be consulted depending on the types of gateways being deployed and the security domains involved. For example, the ‘Cross Domain Solutions’ section of these guidelines should be consulted for gateways between different security domains where at least one security domain is classified SECRET or TOP SECRET.

Personnel involved in the planning, design, implementation or assessment of gateways should also refer to the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) [Gateway security guidance package](#3df21b72-0559-4bbf-a990-5ee509f70747) and, where applicable, the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a) and its associated [Australian Government Gateway Security Standard](#2d003129-793e-4263-92ee-f6d02d016023).

# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on the procurement of outsourced services can be found in the ‘Managed services and cloud services’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on designing, configuring and managing networks can be found in the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on privileged access to systems can be found in the ‘Access to systems and their resources’ section of the [Guidelines for personnel security](#7d16ae67-87a7-4861-b939-e13ec279b5a2).

Further information on cyber security awareness training can be found in the ‘Cyber security awareness training’ section of the [Guidelines for personnel security](#7d16ae67-87a7-4861-b939-e13ec279b5a2).

Further information on authenticating users can be found in the ‘Authentication hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on authenticating IT equipment can be found in the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on [RPKI](#7da35efd-7196-4aba-bd79-30c19e8ef48b) and [ROA records](#355b47b5-6253-4be5-ba7c-274833d84b75) is available from the Asia Pacific Network Information Centre.

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c).

Further information on event logging for network devices in gateways can also be found in ASD’s [Priority logs for SIEM ingestion: Practitioner guidance](#1dbda98a-4e8b-4a52-b4f7-9d1a895fd324) publication.

Further information on [the purpose of IRAP](#84d3cbab-ab84-4981-a4f7-2bd469c8deaf) is available from ASD."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-implementing-gateways","sequence":15790},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-system-administrators-for-g","sequence":15860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-system-administration-of-ga","sequence":15890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-authenticating-to-networks-","sequence":15920},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-border-gateway-protocol-rou","sequence":15950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-gateway-event-logging","sequence":15970},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-assessment-of-gateways","sequence":16010}],"title":"Gateways","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Assessment of gateways","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Testing of gateways following configuration changes, and at regular intervals no more than six months apart, can assist in validating that gateways conform to expected security configurations. In addition, gateways will need to undergo regular security assessments by an Infosec Registered Assessor Program (IRAP) assessor, or an ASD assessor (or their delegate), to determine their security posture and security risks associated with their use. Following an initial security assessment, subsequent security assessments should focus on any new services that are being offered as well as any security-related changes that have occurred since the previous security assessment."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-assessment-of-gateways","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1037","sequence":15980},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0100","sequence":15990},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2019","sequence":16000}],"title":"Assessment of gateways","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Authenticating to networks accessed via gateways","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Ensuring users and information technology (IT) equipment are authenticated to other networks accessed via gateways can reduce the likelihood of unauthorised access."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-authenticating-to-networks-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0619","sequence":15900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0622","sequence":15910}],"title":"Authenticating to networks accessed via gateways","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Gateway event logging","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Centrally logging and analysing security-relevant events, including configuration changes, for gateways can assist in monitoring the security posture of gateways, detecting malicious behaviour and contributing to investigations following cyber security incidents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-gateway-event-logging","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0634","sequence":15960}],"title":"Gateway event logging","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"System administration of gateways","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"In performing administrative activities for gateways, it is important that they are conducted via a secure path isolated from all connected networks. In doing so, this will minimise threats should a connected network be compromised by malicious actors. Furthermore, where gateways exist between networks belonging to different security domains, any shared components should be managed by system administrators for the higher security domain, alternatively, it may be more appropriate to use system administrators from a mutually agreed upon third party."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-gateways-system-administration-of-ga","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1774","sequence":15870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0629","sequence":15880}],"title":"System administration of gateways","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using peripheral switches","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When accessing different systems through peripheral switches, it is important that sufficient assurance be obtained in their operation to ensure that data does not pass between connected systems. As such, the level of assurance needed in peripheral switches is determined by the difference in sensitivity or classification of systems they are connected to.\n\nThere is no requirement for evaluated peripheral switches to be used when all connected systems belong to the same security domain."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-peripheral-switches-using-peripheral","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0591","sequence":16730},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1457","sequence":16740},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1480","sequence":16750}],"title":"Using peripheral switches","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using web application firewalls","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When using a web application firewall (WAF), care should be taken with their configuration to ensure that the IP addresses of an organisation’s web servers (referred to as origin servers) are not identifiable by malicious actors, as knowledge of origin server IP addresses could allow for protections provided by a WAF to be bypassed. Additionally, appropriate controls should be applied to only enable communication between origin servers, the WAF and authorised management networks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-application-firewalls-using-web-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1862","sequence":16210}],"title":"Using web application firewalls","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Web content filters","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on content filtering techniques can be found in the ‘Content filtering’ section of these guidelines.

Further information and [examples of client-side JavaScript controls](#6b4353e0-7281-49f4-a520-f35a4cceec71) are available from the NoScript project."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-using-web-conten","sequence":16380},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-transport-layer-","sequence":16400},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-allowing-and-blo","sequence":16440}],"title":"Web content filters","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Allowing and blocking access to domain names","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Defining an organisation-approved list of domain names, and blocking all others, removes one of the most common data exfiltration paths used by malicious actors. In doing so, even a relatively permissive list of allowed domain names, such as the entire Australian top-level domain (‘\\*.au’) or the top 1,000 websites from the Alexa website ranking, offers better security than relying solely on a list of malicious domain names.\n\nFurthermore, in cases where an organisation chooses to implement a relatively permissive list of allowed domain names, or list of website categories, security risks can be further mitigated by blocking dynamic domain names, or domain names that can be registered anonymously for free, as these are often used by malicious actors due to their lack of attribution. Finally, as users rarely have a requirement to access websites via their IP addresses instead of their domain names, the presence of such activities could indicate malicious code attempting to communicate with malicious actors’ command and control infrastructure and should be blocked."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-allowing-and-blo","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0958","sequence":16410},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1236","sequence":16420},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1171","sequence":16430}],"title":"Allowing and blocking access to domain names","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Transport Layer Security filtering","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As encrypted Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure connections can bypass traditional web content filtering techniques, an organisation should implement Transport Layer Security (TLS) inspection. However, an organisation may choose to allow some web traffic, such as that for internet banking, to go uninspected to protect the privacy of users."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-transport-layer-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0263","sequence":16390}],"title":"Transport Layer Security filtering","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using web content filters","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Effective web content filters can greatly reduce the likelihood of malicious code, or other inappropriate content, being accessed by users. Furthermore, web content filters can disrupt or prevent malicious actors from communicating with their malicious code if they manage to deploy it on an organisation’s networks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-gateways-web-content-filters-using-web-conten","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0963","sequence":16350},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0961","sequence":16360},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1237","sequence":16370}],"title":"Using web content filters","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Degaussing magnetic media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Degaussing magnetic media changes its magnetic properties, permanently corrupting data. When degaussing magnetic media, care needs to be taken as a degausser of insufficient magnetic field strength will not be effective. In addition, since 2006 perpendicular magnetic media has progressively replaced longitudinal magnetic media. As some older degaussers are only capable of destroying longitudinal magnetic media, care needs to be taken to ensure that a degausser with a suitable magnetic orientation is also used. Furthermore, to ensure that degaussers are being used in the correct manner to effectively destroy magnetic media, product-specific directions provided by degausser manufacturers should be followed. Finally, to provide an additional level of assurance following the use of a degausser, magnetic media should be physically damaged by deforming any internal platters."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-destruction-degaussing-magnetic-m","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0361","sequence":7120},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0362","sequence":7130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1641","sequence":7140}],"title":"Degaussing magnetic media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Outsourcing media destruction","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"While media storing accountable material cannot be outsourced, media storing non-accountable material can be outsourced when using a National Association for Information Destruction AAA certified destruction service with endorsements, as specified in ASIO’s Protective Security Circular-167, External destruction of security classified information. This publication is available from the Protective Security Policy GovTEAMS community or ASIO by email."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-destruction-outsourcing-media-des","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0839","sequence":7220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0840","sequence":7230}],"title":"Outsourcing media destruction","version":"2026.06.18"}

As such, its destruction should be supervised by at least two cleared personnel who sign a destruction certificate afterwards."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-destruction-supervision-of-accoun","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0372","sequence":7190},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0373","sequence":7200}],"title":"Supervision of accountable material destruction","version":"2026.06.18"}

However, if a destruction method is used that results in the generation of media waste particles, such as a hammer mill, disintegrator, grinder/sander or by cutting, it may still need to be stored and handled as classified waste."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-destruction-treatment-of-media-wa","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1728","sequence":7090},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1729","sequence":7100}],"title":"Treatment of media waste particles","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Disposal of media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Before media can be released into the public domain, it needs to be sanitised, destroyed or declassified. As sanitised, destroyed or declassified media still presents a security risk, albeit minor, an appropriate authority needs to formally authorise its release into the public domain. Furthermore, as part of disposal processes, removing labels and markings indicating the owner, sensitivity, classification or any other marking that can associate media with its prior use will ensure it does not draw undue attention following its disposal."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-disposal-disposal-of-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0378","sequence":7280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0375","sequence":7290}],"title":"Disposal of media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Hybrid hard drives

When sanitising hybrid hard drives, separate the non-volatile magnetic media from the circuit board containing non-volatile flash memory media and sanitise each separately.

# Solid-state drives

When sanitising solid-state drives, the method for sanitising non-volatile flash memory media applies.

# Further information

Further information on the random-access memory testing tool [MemTest86](#629818d8-430c-4125-8fd2-5e3f988fce21) can be obtained from PassMark Software.

Further information on the graphics card random-access memory testing tools [MemtestG80](#39e186f3-d175-4eed-afe6-2b9a7a936a91) and [MemtestCL](#9d38f42d-eeff-4124-93db-4b29abaafb67) can be obtained from their GitHub projects.

Further information on HDDerase is available from the [Center for Memory and Recording Research](#401ed034-e2bd-43b4-9fde-05d07618039a) at the University of California San Diego. HDDerase can call the ATA secure erase command as well as reset the host-protected area and device configuration overlay table on non-volatile magnetic media."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-media-sanitisation-p","sequence":6680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-volatile-media-sanit","sequence":6710},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-volatil","sequence":6730},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-magneti","sequence":6770},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-non-vol","sequence":6790},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-erasabl","sequence":6810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-electri","sequence":6830},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-non-vol","sequence":6850},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-flash-m","sequence":6870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-non-vol","sequence":6890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-media-that-cannot-be","sequence":6910}],"title":"Media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Media sanitisation processes and procedures","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using approved methods to sanitise media provides a level of assurance that, to the extent possible, no data will be left following sanitisation. The methods described in these guidelines are designed not only to prevent common data recovery practices but also to protect from those that could emerge in the future."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-media-sanitisation-p","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0348","sequence":6670}],"title":"Media sanitisation processes and procedures","version":"2026.06.18"}
