For example, due to the media being faulty or damaged. In such cases, the media will need to be destroyed prior to its disposal."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-media-that-cannot-be","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1735","sequence":6900}],"title":"Media that cannot be successfully sanitised","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Non-volatile electrically erasable programmable read-only memory media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A single overwrite with a random pattern, followed by a read back for verification, is considered suitable for sanitising non-volatile electrically erasable programmable read-only memory (EEPROM) media."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-electri","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0836","sequence":6820}],"title":"Non-volatile electrically erasable programmable read-only memory media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Non-volatile erasable programmable read-only memory media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When sanitising non-volatile erasable programmable read-only memory (EPROM), three times the manufacturer’s specification for ultraviolet erasure time should be applied to provide additional certainty in sanitisation processes. Subsequently, the non-volatile EPROM media should be overwritten at least once in its entirety with a random pattern followed by a read back for verification."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-erasabl","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0357","sequence":6800}],"title":"Non-volatile erasable programmable read-only memory media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Non-volatile flash memory media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"For non-volatile flash memory media, a technique known as wear levelling ensures that writes are distributed evenly across each memory block. This feature necessitates non-volatile flash memory media being overwritten with a random pattern at least twice, and followed by a read back for verification, as this helps to ensure that all memory blocks are overwritten."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-flash-m","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0359","sequence":6860}],"title":"Non-volatile flash memory media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Non-volatile magnetic media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Non-volatile magnetic media encompasses non-volatile magnetic hard drives, magnetic tape and floppy disks. While non-volatile magnetic tape and floppy disks can be sanitised by overwriting them at least once (or three times if pre-2001 or under 15 GB) in their entirety with a random pattern followed by a read back for verification, additional considerations apply to non-volatile magnetic hard drives due to their use of a host-protected area, device configuration overlay table and growth defects table.

The host-protected area and device configuration overlay table of non-volatile magnetic hard drives are normally not visible to a computer’s Unified Extensible Firmware Interface or operating system. Therefore, any sanitisation of the readable sectors of non-volatile magnetic hard drives will leave any data contained in sectors listed in the host-protected area and device configuration overlay table untouched. Some sanitisation applications include the ability to reset non-volatile magnetic hard drives to their default state, removing any host-protected areas or device configuration overlays. This allows the sanitisation applications to see the entire contents of non-volatile magnetic hard drives during subsequent sanitisation processes.

Modern non-volatile magnetic hard drives automatically reallocate space for bad sectors at a hardware level. These bad sectors are maintained in what is known as the growth defects table or ‘g-list’. If data was stored in a sector that was subsequently added to the growth defects table, sanitising the non-volatile magnetic hard drive will not overwrite such data. While these sectors may be considered bad by non-volatile magnetic hard drives, quite often this is due to the sectors no longer meeting expected performance norms and not due to an inability to read or write to them. The Advanced Technology Attachment (ATA) secure erase command was built into the firmware of post-2001 non-volatile magnetic hard drives and can access sectors that have been added to the growth defects table.

Modern non-volatile magnetic hard drives also contain a primary defects table or ‘p-list’. The primary defects table contains a list of bad sectors found during post-production processes. No data is ever stored in sectors listed in the primary defects table as they are marked as inaccessible before non-volatile magnetic hard drives are used for the first time."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-non-volatile-magneti","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0354","sequence":6740},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1065","sequence":6750},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1067","sequence":6760}],"title":"Non-volatile magnetic media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Treatment of non-volatile flash memory media following sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Due to the use of wear levelling in non-volatile flash memory media, and the potential for bad memory blocks, it is possible that not all memory blocks will be overwritten during sanitisation processes. For this reason, SECRET and TOP SECRET non-volatile flash memory media retains its classification following sanitisation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-non-vol","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0360","sequence":6880}],"title":"Treatment of non-volatile flash memory media following sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Treatment of volatile media following sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Research suggests that short-term remanence effects are likely in volatile media. For example, up to minutes at normal room temperatures and up to hours in extremely cold temperatures. Furthermore, some volatile media can suffer from long-term remanence effects resulting from physical changes due to the continuous storage of static data for extended periods. It is for these reasons that under certain circumstances TOP SECRET volatile media retains its classification following sanitisation.\n\nTypical circumstances preventing the reclassification of TOP SECRET volatile media include a static cryptographic key being stored in the same memory location during every boot of a device, or a static image being displayed on a device and stored in volatile media for a period of months."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-treatment-of-volatil","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0835","sequence":6720}],"title":"Treatment of volatile media following sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Volatile media sanitisation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When sanitising volatile media, the specified time to wait following the removal of power is based on applying a safety factor to the time recommended by research into preventing the recovery of data. In addition to the removal of power, SECRET and TOP SECRET volatile media should be overwritten at least once in its entirety with a random pattern followed by a read back for verification."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-sanitisation-volatile-media-sanit","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0351","sequence":6690},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0352","sequence":6700}],"title":"Volatile media sanitisation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Media usage","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on the protection of media can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on securing media when not in use can be found in the ‘IT equipment and media’ section of the [Guidelines for physical security](#4deea7ea-18a2-4fd7-8b25-0b3603215df2).

Further information on encrypting media can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1).

Further information on using media to transfer data between systems can be found in the ‘Data transfers’ section of the [Guidelines for data transfers](#1eefdaf7-3991-4ba2-9439-79565f508c44)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-media-management-policy","sequence":6410},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-removable-media-usage-polic","sequence":6430},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-removable-media-register","sequence":6450},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-labelling-media","sequence":6470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-classifying-media","sequence":6500},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-reclassifying-media","sequence":6530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-encrypting-media","sequence":6570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-handling-media","sequence":6590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-sanitising-media-before-fir","sequence":6620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-using-media-for-data-transf","sequence":6650}],"title":"Media usage","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Classifying media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Media that is not correctly classified could be stored and handled inappropriately, accessed by personnel who do not have an appropriate security clearance or used with systems it is not authorised to be used with."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-classifying-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0323","sequence":6480},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0337","sequence":6490}],"title":"Classifying media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Handling media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As media can be easily misplaced or stolen, physical handling measures should be put in place to protect the data stored on it."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-handling-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0831","sequence":6580}],"title":"Handling media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Labelling media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Labelling media helps personnel to identify its sensitivity or classification and ensure that appropriate measures are applied to its storage, handling and use.\n\nWhile text-based protective markings are typically used for labelling media, there may be circumstances where colour-based protective markings or other marking schemes need to be used instead. In such cases, the marking scheme will need to be documented and personnel trained in its use."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-labelling-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0332","sequence":6460}],"title":"Labelling media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Media management policy","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Since media can store sensitive or classified data, it is important that a media management policy is developed, implemented and maintained to ensure that all types of media, and the data it stores, is protected in an appropriate manner. In many cases, an organisation’s media management policy will be closely tied to their removable media usage policy."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-media-management-policy","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1549","sequence":6400}],"title":"Media management policy","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Reclassifying media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Some activities may necessitate or allow for a change to the sensitivity or classification of media. For example, when media is connected to a system that lacks a mechanism through which read-only access can be ensured, when media is sanitised or destroyed, or when data stored on media is subject to a sensitivity or classification change."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-reclassifying-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0325","sequence":6510},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0330","sequence":6520}],"title":"Reclassifying media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Removable media register","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Developing, implementing, maintaining and regularly verifying a register of removable media can assist an organisation in tracking and accounting for authorised removable media as well as identifying any unauthorised removable media in use within their organisation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-removable-media-register","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1713","sequence":6440}],"title":"Removable media register","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Removable media usage policy","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Establishing a removable media usage policy can decrease the likelihood and consequence of data spills, data loss and data theft. In doing so, a removable media usage policy will likely cover the following:

- permitted types and uses of removable media
- registration and labelling of removable media
- handling and protection of removable media
- reporting of lost or stolen removable media
- sanitisation or destruction of removable media at the end of its life."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-removable-media-usage-polic","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1359","sequence":6420}],"title":"Removable media usage policy","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Sanitising media before first use","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Sanitising media before first use can assist in reducing cyber supply chain risks, such as new media containing malicious code. In addition, sanitising media before first use in a different security domain can prevent potential data spills from occurring."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-sanitising-media-before-fir","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1600","sequence":6600},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1642","sequence":6610}],"title":"Sanitising media before first use","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using media for data transfers","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"An organisation transferring data between systems belonging to different security domains is strongly encouraged to use write-once media. When done properly, such as using non-rewritable compact discs that have been finalised, this ensures that data from the destination system cannot be accidentally transferred, or maliciously exfiltrated, onto the media and then onto another system. Alternatively, if suitable write-once media is not used, the destination system should have a mechanism through which read-only access can be ensured, such as via a read-only device or hardware write-blocker. However, the use of read-only mechanisms is not immune to failure or compromise. Therefore, rewritable media should still be sanitised following each data transfer.\n\nIt is important to note that for most non-volatile flash memory media, it will be possible to sanitise and reclassify it following a data transfer to allow it to be connected to other systems again. This is not possible for SECRET and TOP SECRET non-volatile flash memory media as it cannot be reclassified following sanitisation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-media-media-usage-using-media-for-data-transf","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0347","sequence":6630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0947","sequence":6640}],"title":"Using media for data transfers","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Network design and configuration","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on secure network design can be found in ASD’s [Foundations for modern defensible architecture](#8a67ffd7-61a4-4773-a1e7-e6b146f51d62) publication.

Further information on wireless networks can be found in the ‘Wireless networks’ section of these guidelines.

Further information on gateways can be found in the ‘Gateways’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on encrypting communications can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1).

Further information on network segmentation and segregation can be found in ASD’s [Implementing network segmentation and segregation](#23d977b2-e64e-4ebc-a59f-cd95212045c5) publication.

Further information on network security zones can be found in Canada’s Canadian Centre for Cyber Security’s [Baseline security requirements for network security zones (version 2.0)](#b43b0377-c2e4-4cdc-acec-a9ece7371e36) publication.

Further information on implementing network segmentation and segregation for system administration purposes can be found in the ‘System administration’ section of the [Guidelines for system management](#c6ca6620-ccd5-4c5d-b97c-9d92f1162948).

Further information on functional separation of servers using virtualisation can be found in the ‘Virtualisation hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on blocking anonymity network traffic can be found in ASD’s [Defending against the malicious use of the Tor network](#ded13757-3c76-40cb-b46e-f84e46a1c1c7) publication.

Further information on DNS services can be found in ASD’s [Domain Name System security for domain owners](#b66f8d23-4209-4775-a1fd-bd4dc299a027) and [Domain Name System security for domain resolvers](#b13cb68a-a94a-4a69-b094-b1b68d10239e) publications.

Further information on implementing encrypted DNS can be can be found in the United States’ National Security Agency’s [Adopting Encrypted DNS in Enterprise Environments](#e2205e57-2bf0-4696-8bd8-94a5a5d5c4f2) publication and the Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Encrypted DNS Implementation Guidance](#fba4bdb9-82be-47a4-8198-3d47d5786932) publication.

Further information on selecting a protective DNS service can be found in the United States’ National Security Agency and Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Selecting a Protective DNS Service](#63b0d043-b6b3-4e2b-bca8-99b42cfd9904) publication.

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on network device hardening, particularly for edge devices, can be found in the following publications:

- ASD’s [Mitigation strategies for edge devices: Executive guidance](#b79c4fe8-4541-401f-a32e-87fd83021c98)
- ASD’s [Mitigation strategies for edge devices: Practitioner guidance](#0cae2aee-3762-482f-8ffe-850e98d7d605)
- Canada’s Canadian Centre for Cyber Security’s [Security considerations for edge devices](#c47ab307-e25d-43e7-9700-7b26d9c229c3)
- United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Guidance on digital forensics and protective monitoring specifications for producers of network devices and appliances](#53d281fa-1f25-4d33-9d6b-a5831851df50).

Further information on network device hardening can also be found in the United States’ National Security Agency’s [Network Infrastructure Security Guide](#1ecfc41e-f6a8-4c96-ac41-1587718ca459) publication.

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c).

Further information on event logging for network devices can also be found in ASD’s [Priority logs for SIEM ingestion: Practitioner guidance](#1dbda98a-4e8b-4a52-b4f7-9d1a895fd324) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13440},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13470},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","sequence":13530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-f","sequence":13550},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13570},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-f","sequence":13600},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13620},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","sequence":13680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13710},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13730},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","sequence":13750},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","sequence":13780},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","sequence":13810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-b","sequence":13840},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-e","sequence":13860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-p","sequence":13880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-f","sequence":13900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-d","sequence":13920},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-d","sequence":13940},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-r","sequence":13960},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","sequence":13990}],"title":"Network design and configuration","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Blocking anonymity network traffic","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Inbound network connections from anonymity networks, such as the Tor network, can be used by malicious actors for reconnaissance and malicious code delivery purposes with minimal risk of detection and attribution. As such, this network traffic should be blocked. However, an organisation might choose to support anonymous connections to their websites to cater for individuals who want to remain anonymous for privacy reasons. In such cases, it is suggested that network traffic from anonymity networks be logged and monitored instead. Additionally, outbound network connections to anonymity networks can be used by malicious code for command and control or data exfiltration purposes and should be blocked."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-b","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1627","sequence":13820},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1628","sequence":13830}],"title":"Blocking anonymity network traffic","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Disabling unused physical ports on network devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Disabling unused physical ports on network devices reduces the opportunity for malicious actors to connect to networks if they can gain physical access to network devices."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-d","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0534","sequence":13930}],"title":"Disabling unused physical ports on network devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Encrypted Domain Name System Services","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical naming system built on a distributed database for resources connected to the internet. In performing this service, DNS maps human-readable domain names to their associated IP addresses. Unfortunately, malicious actors can surveil standard DNS requests for intelligence gathering. As such, it is important that DNS traffic is suitably encrypted by clients and servers using ASD-approved cryptography, such as via DNS over Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure or DNS over Transport Layer Security."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-e","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2017","sequence":13850}],"title":"Encrypted Domain Name System Services","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Flashing network devices with trusted firmware before first use","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Flashing network devices with trusted firmware, obtained from vendors via trusted means, before network devices are used for the first time can assist in reducing cyber supply chain risks, such as the introduction of malicious firmware resulting from a cyber supply chain interdiction attack or a compromised vendor development environment or authoritative source for software."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-f","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1800","sequence":13890}],"title":"Flashing network devices with trusted firmware before first use","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Network device event logging","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Centrally logging and analysing security-relevant events, including configuration changes, for network devices, especially internet-facing network devices, can assist in monitoring the security posture of systems, detecting malicious behaviour and contributing to investigations following cyber security incidents."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-n","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1963","sequence":13970},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1964","sequence":13980}],"title":"Network device event logging","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protective Domain Name System Services","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A protective DNS service can be an effective way of blocking requests made by an organisation’s users, or malicious actors on an organisation’s network, to known malicious domain names – either as part of an initial compromise or subsequent command and control activities. DNS event logs captured by a protective DNS service can also be useful for investigating any exploitation attempt or successful compromise of a network by malicious actors.\n\nIn selecting a protective DNS service, many commercial offerings exist. In addition, ASD offers a free protective DNS service for Australia’s most critical systems."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-p","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1782","sequence":13870}],"title":"Protective Domain Name System Services","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Regularly restarting network devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Implementing measures to restart network devices at least monthly can assist in maintaining network device performance as well as removing malicious actors that may have compromised a network device but failed to gain persistence."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-r","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1801","sequence":13950}],"title":"Regularly restarting network devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Using Network-based Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A Network-based Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) or Network-based Intrusion Prevention System (NIPS) can be an effective way of identifying and responding to network intrusions. In addition, generating event logs and alerts for network traffic that contravenes any rule in a firewall ruleset can help identify suspicious or malicious network traffic entering networks due to a failure of, or configuration change to, firewalls."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-network-design-and-configuration-u","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1028","sequence":13790},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1030","sequence":13800}],"title":"Using Network-based Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems","version":"2026.06.18"}

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on the use of cloud service providers can be found in the ‘Managed services and cloud services’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on business continuity and disaster recovery planning can be found in the ‘Chief information security officer’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security roles](#626dab35-81ab-45fe-8c12-0faff1c23c07).

Further information on mitigating denial-of-service attacks can be found in ASD’s [Preparing for and responding to denial-of-service attacks](#c54cc4db-22f4-4d10-8977-2ce62b647bd2) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-service-continuity-for-online-serv","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-service-continuity-for-online-serv","sequence":14420},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-service-continuity-for-online-serv","sequence":14460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-service-continuity-for-online-serv","sequence":14490},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-service-continuity-for-online-serv","sequence":14530}],"title":"Service continuity for online services","version":"2026.06.18"}

{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Wireless networks","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to wireless networks

This section describes the controls applicable to wireless networks and extends upon the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of these guidelines.

# Further information

Further information on [Wi-Fi technologies and associated testing and certification programs](#a9b6660b-fc4c-4a08-8db6-1130370b6b09) is available from the Wi-Fi Alliance.

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the ‘Cyber supply chain risk management’ section of the [Guidelines for procurement and outsourcing](#f37a4848-0791-4870-b316-5536c2681c28).

Further information on evaluated products can be found in the ‘Evaluated product procurement’ section of the [Guidelines for evaluated products](#a699a3aa-828d-479b-b50b-98127bb19437).

Further information on encrypting communications can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-choosing-wireles","sequence":14020},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-public-wireless-","sequence":14040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-administrative-i","sequence":14060},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-default-settings","sequence":14110},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-media-access-con","sequence":14130},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-static-addressin","sequence":14150},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-confidentiality-","sequence":14170},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-802-1x-authentic","sequence":14200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-evaluation-of-80","sequence":14220},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-generating-and-i","sequence":14260},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-caching-802-1x-a","sequence":14280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-fast-basic-servi","sequence":14300},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-remote-authentic","sequence":14320},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-interference-bet","sequence":14340},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-protecting-manag","sequence":14360},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-wireless-network","sequence":14390}],"title":"Wireless networks","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"802.1X authentication","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"WPA3-Enterprise uses 802.1X authentication that requires the use of an Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP). Multiple EAP methods supported by WPA2 and WPA3 are available.\n\nExtensible Authentication Protocol-Transport Layer Security (EAP-TLS) is considered one of the most secure EAP methods and is widely supported. It uses a Public Key Infrastructure to secure communications between devices and a Remote Access Dial-In User Service (RADIUS) server by using X.509 certificates. While EAP-TLS provides strong mutual authentication, it requires an organisation to have established a Public Key Infrastructure. This involves deploying their own certificate authority and issuing certificates, or sourcing certificates from a commercial certificate authority, for every device that accesses their wireless networks. While this introduces additional costs and management overheads, the security advantages are significant."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-802-1x-authentic","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1321","sequence":14180},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1711","sequence":14190}],"title":"802.1X authentication","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Administrative interfaces for wireless access points","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Administrative interfaces allow users to modify the configuration and security settings of wireless access points. Often, by default, wireless access points allow users to access administrative interfaces over fixed network connections or wireless network connections. To assist in reducing the attack surface for wireless access points, the administrative interface should be disabled for wireless network connections."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-administrative-i","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1315","sequence":14050}],"title":"Administrative interfaces for wireless access points","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Caching 802.1X authentication outcomes","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When 802.1X authentication is used, a shared secret key known as the Pairwise Master Key (PMK) is generated upon successful authentication of devices. This PMK is then capable of being cached to assist with fast roaming between wireless access points. When devices roam away from wireless access points they have authenticated to, they will not need to perform a full re-authentication should they roam back while the cached PMK remains valid. To further assist with roaming, wireless access points can be configured to pre-authenticate devices to neighbouring wireless access points that devices might roam to. Although requiring full authentication for devices each time they roam between wireless access points is ideal, an organisation can choose to use PMK caching and pre-authentication if they have a business requirement for fast roaming. If PMK caching is used, the PMK caching period should not be set to greater than 1440 minutes (24 hours)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-caching-802-1x-a","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1330","sequence":14270}],"title":"Caching 802.1X authentication outcomes","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Choosing wireless devices","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Using wireless devices, such as wireless access points, wireless adapters and wireless network cards, which have been certified against a Wi-Fi Alliance certification program, provides an organisation with the assurance that they conform to wireless standards and are guaranteed to be interoperable with other wireless devices on wireless networks."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-choosing-wireles","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1314","sequence":14010}],"title":"Choosing wireless devices","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Confidentiality and integrity of wireless network traffic","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"As wireless networks are often capable of being accessed from outside the perimeter of secured spaces, all wireless network traffic requires suitable cryptographic protection. For this purpose, it is recommended that Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3) be used as it provides equivalent or greater security than Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2). WPA3 has also prohibited the use of various outdated and insecure cipher suites.\n\nWPA3-Enterprise supports three enterprise modes of operation: enterprise only mode, transition mode and 192-bit mode. Preference is given to WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode as this mode ensures no cryptographic algorithms with known weaknesses are used. However, if any other WPA3-Enterprise modes are used then Authentication and Key Management suite 00-0F-AC:1 should be disabled (if this option is available)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-confidentiality-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1332","sequence":14160}],"title":"Confidentiality and integrity of wireless network traffic","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Evaluation of 802.1X authentication implementation","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The security of 802.1X authentication is dependent on four main elements and how they interact. These four elements include supplicants, authenticators, wireless access points and authentication servers. To provide assurance that these elements have been implemented correctly, they should have completed an evaluation."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-evaluation-of-80","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1322","sequence":14210}],"title":"Evaluation of 802.1X authentication implementation","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Fast Basic Service Set Transition","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"The WPA3 standard specifies support for Fast Basic Service Set Transition (FT) (802.11r). FT is a feature designed to improve user mobility and combat lag introduced by the need to authenticate to each wireless access point. However, FT requires authenticators to request and send keys to other authenticators within a security domain. If any of these keys are intercepted, all security properties are lost. Therefore, it is imperative that communications are appropriately secured. As such, FT should be disabled unless it can be confirmed that authenticator-to-authenticator communications are secured by a suitable ASD-Approved Cryptographic Protocol that provides confidentiality, integrity and mutual authentication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-fast-basic-servi","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1712","sequence":14290}],"title":"Fast Basic Service Set Transition","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Generating and issuing certificates for authentication","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When issuing certificates to devices to access wireless networks, an organisation should be aware that certificates could be stolen by malicious code. Once compromised, certificates could be used on other devices to gain unauthorised access to wireless networks. An organisation should also be aware that in only issuing certificates to devices, any actions taken by users will only be attributable to specific devices.\n\nWhen issuing certificates to users to access wireless networks, it can be in the form of certificates that are stored on devices or certificates that are stored on smart cards. While issuing certificates that are stored on smart cards provides increased security, it comes at a higher cost. However, users are more likely to notice missing smart cards and alert their security team, who are then able to revoke their credentials, which can minimise the time malicious actors have access to wireless networks. In addition, to reduce the likelihood of stolen smart cards from being used to gain unauthorised access to wireless networks, multi-factor authentication can be implemented by requiring the use of passwords to access certificates stored on smart cards. This is particularly important when smart cards grant users any form of administrative access."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-generating-and-i","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1324","sequence":14230},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1323","sequence":14240},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1327","sequence":14250}],"title":"Generating and issuing certificates for authentication","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Interference between wireless networks","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When wireless networks are deployed in proximity, there is the potential for interference to impact their availability, especially when operating on commonly used 802.11b/g (2.4 GHz) default channels of 1 and 11. Sufficiently separating wireless networks by using frequency separation can help reduce this security risk. This can be achieved by using wireless networks that are configured to operate on channels that minimise overlapping frequencies, such as using 802.11b/g (2.4 GHz) channels and 802.11n (5 GHz) channels. It is important to note though, if implementing a mix of 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz channels, not all devices may be compatible with 802.11n and able to connect to 5 GHz channels."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-interference-bet","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1334","sequence":14330}],"title":"Interference between wireless networks","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Media Access Control address filtering","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Devices that connect to wireless networks generally have a unique Media Access Control (MAC) address. Using MAC address filtering can prevent rogue devices from connecting to wireless networks. However, malicious actors may be able to determine MAC addresses of legitimate devices and use this information to gain access to wireless networks. As such, MAC address filtering introduces management overhead without any tangible security benefit."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-media-access-con","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1320","sequence":14120}],"title":"Media Access Control address filtering","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Protecting management frames on wireless networks","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"An effective denial-of-service attack can be performed by exploiting unprotected management frames using inexpensive commercial hardware. The 802.11 standard provides no protection for management frames and therefore does not protect against spoofing or denial-of-service attacks. However, the 802.11w amendment specifically addresses the protection of management frames on wireless networks and should be enabled for WPA2. In WPA3, this feature is built into the standard."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-protecting-manag","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1335","sequence":14350}],"title":"Protecting management frames on wireless networks","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Public wireless networks","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"When an organisation provides a public wireless network for public use, connecting the public wireless network to, or sharing infrastructure with, any other organisation networks can create an entry point for malicious actors enabling them to target organisation networks to steal data or disrupt services."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-public-wireless-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0536","sequence":14030}],"title":"Public wireless networks","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service authentication","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Separate to the 802.1X authentication process is the RADIUS authentication process that occurs between authenticators and a RADIUS server. RADIUS is what is known as an authentication, authorisation and accounting protocol, and is intended to mediate network access. However, RADIUS is not secure enough to be used without protection. To protect credentials communicated between authenticators and a RADIUS server, communications should be encapsulated with an additional layer of encryption, such as RADIUS over Internet Protocol Security or RADIUS over Transport Layer Security."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-remote-authentic","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1454","sequence":14310}],"title":"Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service authentication","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Static addressing","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Assigning static IP addresses for devices accessing wireless networks can prevent rogue devices connecting to wireless networks from being assigned routable IP addresses. However, malicious actors may be able to determine IP addresses of legitimate devices and use this information to gain access to wireless networks. As such, configuring devices to use static IP addresses introduces management overhead without any tangible security benefit."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-static-addressin","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1319","sequence":14140}],"title":"Static addressing","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Wireless network footprint","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Instead of deploying a small number of wireless access points that broadcast on high power, a greater number of wireless access points that use less broadcast power can be deployed to achieve the desired footprint for wireless networks. This has the benefit of providing service continuity should wireless access points become unserviceable. In such cases, the output power of nearby wireless access points can be increased to cover the footprint gap until the unserviceable wireless access points can be replaced.\n\nIn addition to minimising the output power of wireless access points to reduce the footprint of wireless networks, the use of radio frequency (RF) shielding can be used for an organisation’s facilities. While expensive, this will limit wireless communications to areas under the control of an organisation. RF shielding on an organisation’s facilities also has the added benefit of preventing the jamming of wireless networks from outside of the facilities in which wireless networks are operating."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-networking-wireless-networks-wireless-network","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1338","sequence":14370},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1013","sequence":14380}],"title":"Wireless network footprint","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Access to systems and their resources","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Security clearances

Where these guidelines refer to security clearances, this includes both Australian security clearances and those from foreign governments that are formally recognised by Australia.

# Further information

Further information on access to government resources, including required security clearances, can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on access to highly sensitive government resources, including required briefings, can be found in the Government Security Committee’s Australian Government Security Caveat Guidelines. This publication is available from the Protective Security Policy GovTEAMS community or the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation by email.

Further information on restricting the use of privileged user accounts can be found in ASD’s [Restricting administrative privileges](#3ccea9a8-a728-4f5b-a0a8-43f2f206f76b) publication.

Further information on administering systems and their resources can be found in the ‘System administration’ section of the [Guidelines for system management](#c6ca6620-ccd5-4c5d-b97c-9d92f1162948).

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":2860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":2880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":2900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":2950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3000},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3040},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3170},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3260},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3280},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3310},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3380},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-access-to-systems-and-thei","sequence":3410}],"title":"Access to systems and their resources","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cyber security awareness training","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on telephone system usage can be found in the ‘Telephone systems’ section of the [Guidelines for communications systems](#3a7b903b-ed0d-450d-9123-60f6f8fd5dd2).

Further information on multifunction device usage can be found in the ‘Multifunction devices’ section of the [Guidelines for communications systems](#3a7b903b-ed0d-450d-9123-60f6f8fd5dd2).

Further information on mobile device usage can be found in the ‘Mobile device usage’ section of the [Guidelines for enterprise mobility](#9a8f5a63-bcd5-45a8-9f9c-974fc796ac95).

Further information on removable media usage can be found in the ‘Media usage’ section of the [Guidelines for media](#b594c9c0-b42f-4f06-b643-38023275a5c7).

Further information on email usage can be found in the ‘Email usage’ section of the [Guidelines for email](#ab73fa95-f213-4041-915e-a8ccbdf74331).

Further information on web usage can be found in the ‘Web proxies’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on detecting socially engineered messages be found in the Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) [Detecting socially engineered messages](#efddc783-51dc-48ac-a53e-a5dd56c17fd0) publication.

Further information on business email compromise can be found in ASD’s [Protecting against business email compromise](#98951624-a06e-4d9a-96a3-f89ac944bcaa) publication.

Further information on the use of social media can be found in ASD’s [Security tips for social media and messaging apps](#c78caaed-9931-4197-b21e-bc4e9979a51a) publication.

Further information on [reporting cybercrime incidents](#188466f6-be12-49ce-b99a-981e54b1663e) and [reporting cyber security incidents](#626d3582-3caf-49d6-89d5-4b8fdbbf1f31), including ASD’s [limited use obligation](#cae0ac6b-e69b-4359-bfd7-5ee5eff1049d), is available from ASD."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2660},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2700},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2720},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2770},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-personnel-security-cyber-security-awareness-t","sequence":2830}],"title":"Cyber security awareness training","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Facilities and systems","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on the certification and accreditation authorities for physical security can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on the physical security requirements for specific security zones can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on selecting security zones and security containers for the protection of information technology (IT) equipment can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on emanation security considerations associated with usage of RF devices in SECRET and TOP SECRET areas can be found in the ‘Emanation security’ section of the [Guidelines for communications infrastructure](#4defa9a8-c5f5-4b39-8b0a-537f14d9d14d).

Further information on medical devices approved by the Therapeutic Goods Administration for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes can be found on the [Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods](#8edb7798-5d97-4811-a8ba-add3d5a123cf)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-phys","sequence":2350},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-phys","sequence":2420},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-phys","sequence":2440},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-brin","sequence":2480},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-brin","sequence":2510},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-brin","sequence":2550},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-prev","sequence":2570}],"title":"Facilities and systems","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Bringing medical devices into facilities","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Medical devices are devices approved by the Therapeutic Goods Administration under the [Therapeutic Goods (Medical Devices) Regulations 2002](#55852ef6-7f16-4559-8235-5014c3c3fa14) for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. The use of medical devices in SECRET and TOP SECRET areas requires active management, similar to RF devices, as they may contain communications functionality that could compromise the security of SECRET or TOP SECRET areas or systems within such areas."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-brin","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2007","sequence":2520},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2008","sequence":2530},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2009","sequence":2540}],"title":"Bringing medical devices into facilities","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Physical access to network devices in public areas","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Unprotected network devices in public areas could lead to accidental or deliberate physical damage resulting in an interruption of services. Alternatively, unauthorised access to network devices may allow malicious actors to reset them to factory default settings or connect directly to them to bypass network access controls. Even if access to network devices is not gained by resetting them to factory default settings, it is highly likely that it will cause an interruption of services.\n\nPhysical access to network devices can be restricted through the implementation of physical security, such as using enclosures that prevent access to their console ports and factory reset buttons, mounting them on ceilings or behind walls, or securing them in security containers."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-phys","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1296","sequence":2430}],"title":"Physical access to network devices in public areas","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Preventing observation by unauthorised people","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Without sufficient perimeter security, the inside of a facility is often observable by unauthorised people, such as via direct observation or by using equipment with a telephoto lens. Ensuring systems, in particular workstation displays and keyboards, are not visible through windows, such as via the use of blinds, curtains, privacy films or workstation positioning, will assist in reducing this security risk."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-facilities-and-systems-prev","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0164","sequence":2560}],"title":"Preventing observation by unauthorised people","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"IT equipment and media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on the handling of IT equipment can be found in the ‘IT equipment usage’ section of the [Guidelines for information technology equipment](#db2e237c-2dbc-4a1f-bae7-9cb63a1f1dc3).

Further information on the handling of media can be found in the ‘Media usage’ section of the [Guidelines for media](#b594c9c0-b42f-4f06-b643-38023275a5c7).

Further information on encrypting media can be found in the ‘Cryptographic fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1).

Further information on selecting security zones and security containers for the protection of IT equipment can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-it-equipment-and-media","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-it-equipment-and-media-secu","sequence":2600}],"title":"IT equipment and media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Securing IT equipment and media","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"IT equipment and media need to be secured when not in use. This can be achieved by implementing one of the following approaches:

- securing IT equipment and media in an appropriate security container
- using IT equipment without hard drives and sanitising memory at shut down
- encrypting hard drives of IT equipment and sanitising memory at shut down
- sanitising memory of IT equipment at shut down and removing and securing any hard drives.

If none of the above approaches are feasible, an organisation may wish to minimise the potential impact of not securing IT equipment when not in use. This can be achieved by preventing sensitive or classified data from being stored on hard drives, storing user profiles and documents on network shares, removing temporary user data at logoff, scrubbing virtual memory at shut down, and sanitising memory at shut down. However, there is no guarantee such measures will always work effectively or will not be bypassed due to unexpected circumstances, such as the loss of power. Therefore, hard drives in such cases will retain their sensitivity or classification for the purposes of reuse, reclassification, declassification, sanitisation, destruction and disposal."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-physical-security-it-equipment-and-media-secu","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0161","sequence":2590}],"title":"Securing IT equipment and media","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Cyber supply chain risk management","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can be found in the following Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) publications:

- [Choosing secure and verifiable technologies: Executive guidance](#f41c5403-9070-41ab-968e-cf69ac8bf34a)
- [Choosing secure and verifiable technologies](#adc62d2d-87b9-43a0-ae76-6e6828ff3bbb)
- [Cyber supply chain risk management](#37f65b5b-ce8c-4137-9c84-41678af1e70e)
- [Identifying cyber supply chain risks](#85ee803a-b711-443b-98bf-b279de8fbed0).

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can also be found in the following publications:

- Canada’s Canadian Centre for Cyber Security’s [Cyber supply chain: An approach to assessing risk](#971c6452-6e97-4b4f-b5e4-64e457cb8320)
- New Zealand’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Supply Chain Cyber Security: In Safe Hands](#8a0daed2-8840-443f-a83e-68252d2dcdc5)
- United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Supply chain security guidance](#19947ae5-678c-4dc0-a35e-283e4af1284c)
- United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Secure by Demand: Priority Considerations for Operational Technology Owners and Operators when Selecting Digital Products](#ab5852b2-0004-4565-8e81-e769ca47ea81).

Further information on cyber supply chain risk management can also be found on the United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [ICT Supply Chain Resource Library](#014064b7-5b77-4a79-af6c-79a16f4d0cb2) website.

Further information on cyber supply chain integrity can be found in National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-161 Rev. 1, [Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management Practices for Systems and Organizations](#0d7243fc-65f0-4b4e-9358-bf4f6e0e34d7).

Further information on outsourced products and services can be found in the Department of Home Affairs’ [Protective Security Policy Framework](#92679127-f61d-486a-a93e-df2a26dfb07a).

Further information on the procurement and use of evaluated operating systems, applications and IT equipment can be found in the ‘Evaluated product procurement’ and ‘Evaluated product use’ sections of the [Guidelines for evaluated products](#a699a3aa-828d-479b-b50b-98127bb19437).

Further information on suppliers that have made a pledge to implement Secure by Design and Secure by Default principles and practices can be found on the United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Secure by Design Pledge](#4974639a-f41a-4280-ae06-33d1f13d6083) website."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-cyber-supply-chai","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-cyber-supply-chai","sequence":1650},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-cyber-supply-chai","sequence":1680},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-cyber-supply-chai","sequence":1720},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-cyber-supply-chai","sequence":1760}],"title":"Cyber supply chain risk management","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Managed services and cloud services","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Further information

Further information on the use of outsourced cloud services can be found in the ‘Service continuity for online services’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on the use of outsourced gateway services can be found in the ‘Gateways’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on managed service providers can be found in ASD’s [How to manage your security when engaging a managed service provider](#45899752-c83f-4976-bc99-03f6824a0793) and [Questions to ask managed service providers](#76068c96-8d89-4c37-9eef-03488d5e1b1d) publications.

Further information on the definition of cloud computing can be found in National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-145, [The NIST Definition of Cloud Computing](#c17600e6-8b13-4d6e-a380-035b51a71b73).

Further information on securing cloud services can be found in the following ASD publications:

- [Cloud computing security for cloud service providers](#347eee88-cb61-44b9-81ce-ab97dd08387c)
- [Cloud computing security for executives](#c8ee37e4-fb81-4fa7-8f14-132c850974bc)
- [Cloud computing security for tenants](#b959caf4-8b60-4b82-9552-963754841bb7).

Further information on conducting security assessments of cloud service providers can be found in ASD’s [Cloud assessment and authorisation](#b6479d9a-43c9-41f2-9dbc-2bb689c8e6d8) and [Cloud assessment and authorisation FAQ](#dda788b6-183b-4e89-8254-8c4b5d0540a0) publications.

Further information on shared responsibility models for cloud services can be found in ASD’s [Cloud shared responsibility model: Executive guidance](#2d78eb83-ba21-4fe3-8466-e6d1cd8ee6dc) publication.

Further information on [the purpose of IRAP](#84d3cbab-ab84-4981-a4f7-2bd469c8deaf) is available from ASD.

Further information on reporting cyber security incidents can be found in the ‘Reporting cyber security incidents’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security incidents](#fe0138db-e83b-4a23-85d3-d84e1c22816f)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":1800},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":1830},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":1870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":1900},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":2030},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-procurement-and-outsourcing-managed-services-","sequence":2060}],"title":"Managed services and cloud services","version":"2026.06.18"}
