props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Vulnerability assessments and penetration tests","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"Measures to monitor and manage vulnerabilities in systems can provide an organisation with a wealth of valuable information about their exposure to cyber threats, as well as assisting them to determine security risks associated with the operation of their systems. Regardless of the security assessment activity performed, they should be conducted by suitably skilled personnel augmented by AI models. In doing so, personnel can be internal to an organisation or from a third party, however, such personnel should be independent of the system being assessed. This ensures that there is no conflict of interest, perceived or otherwise, and that the activities are undertaken in an objective manner."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-assessments-vulne","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2118","sequence":11230},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-2119","sequence":11240}],"title":"Vulnerability assessments and penetration tests","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Security monitoring","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Security monitoring activities

These guidelines are intended for security-relevant event logs. They are not intended for non-security-relevant event logs, such as operating system and application performance-related event logs.

# Detecting cyber security events

One of the core elements of detecting cyber security events is the availability of appropriate data sources, such as event logs. The following types of event logs can be used by an organisation to assist in detecting cyber security events:

- **Artificial intelligence (AI) applications:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Cross Domain Solutions:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious network traffic indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Databases:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Domain Name System services:** May assist in identifying attempts to resolve malicious domain names or Internet Protocol addresses indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Email servers:** May assist in identifying users targeted with phishing emails, helping to identify the initial vector of a compromise.
- **Gateways:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious network traffic indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Mobile applications:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Multifunction devices:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious user behaviour indicating a cyber security incident.
- **Operating systems:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious activity indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Remote access services:** May assist in identifying unusual locations of access or times of access indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Security products:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or network traffic indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Server applications:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **System access:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **User applications:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Web applications:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious code or user behaviour indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.
- **Web proxies:** May assist in identifying anomalous or malicious network traffic indicating an exploitation attempt or successful compromise.

# Further information

Further information on logging intrusion activity can be found in the ‘Managing cyber security incidents’ section of the [Guidelines for cyber security incidents](#fe0138db-e83b-4a23-85d3-d84e1c22816f).

Further information on event logging for application-based security products can be found in the ‘Operating system hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on event logging for AI applications can be found in the ‘Software development fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for software development](#506198a8-7ae8-4c95-8b7b-2a4833cfab4b).

Further information on event logging for Cross Domain Solutions can be found in the ‘Cross Domain Solutions’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on event logging for databases can be found in the ‘Databases’ section of the [Guidelines for database systems](#3f349d16-11a1-459a-a299-c9446aea7597).

Further information on event logging for gateways can be found in the ‘Gateways’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on event logging for mobile applications can be found in the ‘Software development fundamentals’ section of the [Guidelines for software development](#506198a8-7ae8-4c95-8b7b-2a4833cfab4b).

Further information on event logging for multifunction devices can be found in the ‘Multifunction devices’ section of the [Guidelines for communications systems](#3a7b903b-ed0d-450d-9123-60f6f8fd5dd2).

Further information on event logging for network-based security products can be found in the ‘Network design and configuration’ section of the [Guidelines for networking](#f145ff5b-d396-4248-8f48-621349d6f0ed).

Further information on event logging for operating systems can be found in the ‘Operating system hardening’ and ‘Authentication hardening’ sections of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on event logging for server applications can be found in the ‘Server application hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on event logging for system access can be found in the ‘Access to systems and their resources’ section of the [Guidelines for personnel security](#7d16ae67-87a7-4861-b939-e13ec279b5a2).

Further information on event logging for user applications can be found in the ‘User application hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on event logging for web applications can be found in the ‘Software development’ section of the [Guidelines for software development](#506198a8-7ae8-4c95-8b7b-2a4833cfab4b).

Further information on event logging for web proxies can be found in the ‘Web proxies’ section of the [Guidelines for gateways](#e8bde527-526c-4a6a-b66f-05228f09dd7b).

Further information on event logging can be found in the following ASD publications:

- [Best practices for event logging and threat detection](#b95c4745-572a-4121-b4e1-d0baa90a84fc)
- [Detecting and mitigating Active Directory compromises](#331263bc-3314-496b-9d35-76788eb0f403)
- [Hardening Microsoft Windows 10 workstations](#9209d9cd-86c1-486f-890e-1edfa4545093)
- [Hardening Microsoft Windows 11 workstations](#43fbcaf9-03a3-493a-83be-9340a37778fa)
- [Priority logs for SIEM ingestion: Practitioner guidance](#1dbda98a-4e8b-4a52-b4f7-9d1a895fd324)
- [Windows event logging and forwarding](#de239dae-d1e8-4969-9680-ef3444d32a83).

Further information on ASD’s cyber threat intelligence service, that is available to [ASD Cyber Security Network Partners](#585f4feb-d589-4a8e-868a-9ae60cf822ea), can be found on ASD’s [Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing service](#b105f0d3-3354-498a-88fe-a05a7362b4b4) webpage.

Further information on using AI models for security monitoring activities can be found in ASD’s [Opportunities for AI in cyber defence](#aff92212-4cd2-459a-9ba1-d4dd1815ebec) publication.

Further information on SIEM and SOAR platforms can be found in ASD’s [Implementing SIEM and SOAR platforms: Executive guidance](#4bebe303-7a91-4ffa-90d8-8df9f80a6318) and [Implementing SIEM and SOAR platforms: Practitioner guidance](#4e01708d-9c2d-4a84-a08a-cb98077235a6) publications.

Further information on prioritising the collection and storage of event logs can be found in the United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Guidance for Implementing M-21-31: Improving the Federal Government's Investigative and Remediation Capabilities](#aeba0a2d-b48a-42f0-b047-622b9fea9a91) publication.

Further information on the National Archives of Australia’s requirements for event log retention can be found in their [AFDA Express Version 2 – Technology \& Information Management](#cc1a55cb-34ab-4418-b660-53571b3af899) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-securi","sequence":10840},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-centra","sequence":10910},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-event-","sequence":10940},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-event-","sequence":11060},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-event-","sequence":11090}],"title":"Security monitoring","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Centralised event logging facility","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"A centralised event logging facility can be used to capture, protect and manage event logs from multiple sources in a coordinated manner. This may be achieved by using a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) platform, a Security Orchestration, Automation and Response (SOAR) platform, or both. Furthermore, in support of a centralised event logging facility, it is important that an accurate and consistent time source is used to assist with identifying connections between events."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-centra","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1405","sequence":10850},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1983","sequence":10860},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1984","sequence":10870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1985","sequence":10880},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1815","sequence":10890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0988","sequence":10900}],"title":"Centralised event logging facility","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set

As such, event logs should be retained for a suitable period to facilitate these activities."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-event-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1988","sequence":11070},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-1989","sequence":11080}],"title":"Event log retention","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value: {"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Security monitoring policy","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"By developing a security monitoring policy, taking into consideration any shared responsibilities between service providers and their customers, an organisation can improve their ability to detect malicious behaviour on their systems. In doing so, the security monitoring policy should cover the types of events to be logged, the event logging facilities to be used, how event logs will be monitored and how long event logs will be retained."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-security-assurance-security-monitoring-securi","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/req/ism-0580","sequence":10830}],"title":"Security monitoring policy","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Artificial intelligence application development","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to artificial intelligence application development

This section describes the controls applicable to AI application and AI model development and extends upon the ‘Software development fundamentals’ section of these guidelines.

# Further information

Further information on large language model application security risks can be found in the [OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications version 2025](#7a40de61-7e8c-4b58-bba0-446899b22b9e) publication.

Further information on AI security risks can be found in the following ASD publications:

- [An introduction to artificial intelligence](#e513ac6b-09fa-4d53-951a-a5142208cf12)
- [Artificial intelligence and machine learning: Supply chain risks and mitigations](#233c7982-dc01-496e-b761-b24ac06ded49)
- [Engaging with artificial intelligence](#0248ace8-2e08-40f8-b19e-117c76909cf1).

Further information on AI security risks can also be found in the following publications:

- MITRE’s [Adversarial Threat Landscape for Artificial-Intelligence Systems](#1258a1ce-774a-4701-8d47-80940b988a2d)
- National Institute of Standards and Technology AI 100-2 E2023, [Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology of Attacks and Mitigations](#9af7c141-51de-43f0-97bc-547e24e96545)
- United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre and United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Guidelines for secure AI system development](#353dcc1a-23e6-48d5-8047-950cc614bf0b)
- United States’ National Security Agency’s [AI Data Security: Best Practices for Securing Data Used to Train \& Operate AI Systems](#6e17dd11-a1c5-4f2b-87df-c5edde5fc8bd)
- United States’ National Security Agency’s [Deploying AI Systems Securely: Best Practices for Deploying Secure and Resilient AI Systems](#c5ca2027-161e-4322-9367-7ec736b4846f)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12270},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12300},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12340},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12380},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12410},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12430},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-artificial-intelligence-","sequence":12450}],"title":"Artificial intelligence application development","version":"2026.06.18"}

This section describes the controls applicable to mobile application development and extends upon the ‘Software development fundamentals’ section of these guidelines.

# Further information

Further information on mobile application security can be found in the [OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard version 2.1.0](#5935bbd2-883f-45e1-861e-e33a1e279063) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-mobile-application-devel","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-mobile-application-devel","sequence":12480}],"title":"Mobile application development","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Software development fundamentals","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to software development

This section applies to human, artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted, AI-powered and AI-driven software development activities for traditional applications (including user applications and server applications), AI applications, mobile applications and web applications. Additional sections of these guidelines should also be consulted depending on the type of software development being undertaken. For example, the ‘Web application development’ section of these guidelines should be consulted for additional controls applicable to web applications. Where references are made to software developers, they apply to humans and AI.

# Further information

Further information on a secure software development framework can be found in National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publication 800-218, [Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1: Recommendations for Mitigating the Risk of Software Vulnerabilities](#6f1f2f2b-abe6-46e4-9c9d-8339f1d9dab7).

Further information on Secure by Design and Secure by Default principles and practices can be found in the following publications:

- ASD’s [Secure by Design foundations](#6494bba4-bdb5-4b19-8f6c-8d5506a8bb09)
- ASD’s [IoT Secure by Design guidance for manufacturers](#0d9d5f12-3519-4585-82d9-bc0f42abcd3c)
- United Kingdom’s National Cyber Security Centre’s [Secure development and deployment guidance](#c74c6e7a-2209-4337-bb3a-715ce6e792a4)
- United Kingdom’s Central Digital and Data Office’s [Secure by Design Principles](#89a4e4d7-0075-40f1-ae05-dff0a19183f9) and [Secure by Design Activities](#9898653e-fc87-4b42-a1fa-f7e323902841)
- United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Safe Software Deployment: How Software Manufacturers Can Ensure Reliability for Customers](#94d9fbcb-e8d1-4ef2-b543-5938f4387fee)
- United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Shifting the Balance of Cybersecurity Risk: Principles and Approaches for Secure by Design Software](#b794a062-2339-48ed-89ac-14aa4c3a61db).

Further information on [secure programming practices](#672e85cc-2d40-46ba-9e94-c604b9731c75) is available from the Carnegie Mellon University’s Software Engineering Institute.

Further information on the need for memory-safe programming languages can be found the following publications:

- United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [The Case for Memory Safe Roadmaps: Why Both C-Suite Executives and Technical Experts Need to Take Memory Safe Coding Seriously](#bdb9d443-a877-4197-b9ad-ec5853f081c7)
- United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Exploring Memory Safety in Critical Open Source Projects](#a9307fb2-8003-4e6e-9d64-2c00ea8b0ec8)
- United States’ National Security Agency’s [Software Memory Safety](#26137381-dc34-485c-8331-3bc9876ed48c)
- United States’ National Security Agency and Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [Memory Safe Languages: Reducing Vulnerabilities in Modern Software Development](#afe3656f-2a78-488f-9eae-3cb9af69b018).

Further information on [cyber supply chain transparency](#d0ace151-3c21-49af-bde7-eda58ab18a82), and recommended content for a software bill of materials, can be found in the United States’ National Telecommunications and Information Administration’s [The Minimum Elements For a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)](#70371e26-f3fb-4b05-85ea-7f6e1f2ee184) publication.

Further information on software bill of materials can also be found in the United States’ Cybersecurity \& Infrastructure Security Agency’s [A Shared Vision of Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for Cybersecurity](#ea6f8148-55ea-4540-8b39-f35cc3411eb2) publication.

Further information on strong authentication can be found in the ‘Authentication hardening’ section of the [Guidelines for system hardening](#de7525f3-a466-40a5-abdd-3ae24a6d1b44).

Further information on software security testing can be found on the Open Worldwide Application Security Project’s (OWASP) [DevSecOps Guidelines](#5516125f-d0eb-4196-8dc4-f8db55fca032) and [Source Code Analysis Tools](#cc618df8-757e-4d6c-9ad2-405554cb487d) websites.

Further information on using AI models for software security testing activities can be found in ASD’s [Opportunities for AI in cyber defence](#aff92212-4cd2-459a-9ba1-d4dd1815ebec) publication.

Further information on vulnerability identification success rates using frontier AI models is available from the [CyberGym](#c81898f4-ae8b-486f-b175-ae8e4dd5e60f) website.

Further information on implementing a vulnerability disclosure program can be found in the following publications:

- Google’s [Starting a Vulnerability Disclosure Program](#78a48231-9963-4f78-962a-861c96c58ead)
- Carnegie Mellon University’s Software Engineering Institute’s [The CERT Guide to Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure](#7ffd20ed-4f40-43be-a37b-97e7a1b31e2f)
- International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 29147:2018, [Information technology – Security techniques – Vulnerability disclosure](#8f1965a9-a6a1-4342-bfb8-797ea358db30)
- International Organization for Standardization/International Electrotechnical Commission 30111:2019, [Information technology – Security techniques – Vulnerability handling processes](#99c117f0-78e0-4196-adc8-705405d996bc).

Further information on [developing a vulnerability disclosure policy](#aab0523a-513e-48c9-978e-32d5b9adeea6) is available from the disclose.io project to assist an organisation with their implementation.

Further information on [recommended contents for a ‘security.txt’ file](#24eb9af8-fb43-4772-a461-2c36dd0f6d7e) is available to assist an organisation with their implementation.

Further information on [reporting vulnerabilities](#03f761fa-7014-459c-aa16-02c303d50abb) to ASD as an independent coordinator, including ASD’s [limited use obligation](#cae0ac6b-e69b-4359-bfd7-5ee5eff1049d), is available from ASD.

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c)."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11310},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11360},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11380},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11450},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11480},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11780},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11810},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11840},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":11940},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12000},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12050},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12110},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12160},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12200},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-software-development-fun","sequence":12220}],"title":"Software development fundamentals","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Web application development","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# Introduction to web application development

This section describes the controls applicable to web application development and extends upon the ‘Software development fundamentals’ section of these guidelines.

# Further information

Further information on web application security can be found in the [OWASP Application Security Verification Standard 5.0.0](#d2f005c5-2ad3-4b5e-9eb1-d0642f3560f4) and [OWASP Top 10 Proactive Controls 2024](#18274424-7086-419e-9fae-a6ba64870459) publications.

Further information on web application security risks can be found in the [OWASP Top 10:2025](#b5b7b7fc-d3c7-4861-a036-a037593c575b) publication.

Further information on implementing HTTPS can be found in ASD’s [Implementing certificates, TLS, HTTPS and opportunistic TLS](#b8cdbf18-31cb-4e10-bb43-e8488ee8c2f6) publication.

Further information on using TLS in HTTPS can be found in the ‘Transport Layer Security’ section of the [Guidelines for cryptography](#195abe87-bb1e-4006-8b42-eaecca4d51f1).

Further information on web API security can be found in the [OWASP API Security Top 10 2023](#76087ab6-88d4-4610-9a98-6ddc5f7bd7e6) publication."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","sequence":12590},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","sequence":12610},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","sequence":12630},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","sequence":12650},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-software-development-web-application-developm","sequence":12670}],"title":"Web application development","version":"2026.06.18"}

props.name: set
props.value:
{"facets":{"https://ns.oscal.org/compat/oscal-1x@1":{"group-parts":[{"group":"Authentication hardening","name":"overview","prose":{"en":"# User accounts and authentication types

The guidance within this section is equally applicable to all user accounts unless specified otherwise. This includes unprivileged user accounts and privileged user accounts, which include break glass accounts and service accounts. In addition, the guidance is equally applicable to interactive authentication and non-interactive authentication.

# Further information

Further information on implementing multi-factor authentication can be found in ASD’s [Implementing multi-factor authentication](#83e3a9b1-5057-4531-91dd-03c8d92634b0) publication.

Further information on event logging can be found in the ‘Security monitoring’ section of the [Guidelines for security assurance](#ef60c0e4-acfb-4a8b-b5ed-25675c6e117c).

Further information on [randomly generating passphrases](#58282062-5c17-476a-98b1-105a627cd28d) is available from the Electronic Frontier Foundation while a [random dice roller](#0508be6f-cb97-44da-b212-42416a0048b0) is available from RANDOM.ORG.

Further information on how to [secure group Managed Service Accounts](#91b92563-d991-40fa-9adc-548df9f6c496) in Microsoft Windows Server is available from Microsoft.

Further information on changing credentials for the Kerberos Key Distribution Center’s service account can be found in Microsoft’s [Active Directory accounts](#ae426d0a-adb6-43b8-a463-faa33e83b679) and [Active Directory Forest Recovery - Reset the krbtgt password](#3e5a98e5-9219-46c8-81c2-e3a4d13407ce) publications. A script for [changing credentials for this service account](#f74ba095-a7f7-4b8c-9e60-5fe84f2a2d0b) is also available from Microsoft.

Further information [memory integrity functionality](#d446dea3-c36d-45af-9623-05b686624af0) is available from Microsoft.

Further information on [Local Security Authority protection functionality](#3f43c8d2-8a8c-4e2f-af80-48a607bce643) is available from Microsoft.

Further information on [Credential Guard functionality](#8d53ee7f-54c2-4380-8408-f7403db30ba1) and [Remote Credential Guard functionality](#92975dff-58e0-4813-842b-f27c0533ca56) is available from Microsoft."}}]}},"id":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening","lifecycle":"active","members":[{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-aut","sequence":9180},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-ins","sequence":9230},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-mul","sequence":9430},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-sin","sequence":9460},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-pas","sequence":9580},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-set","sequence":9640},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-set","sequence":9700},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-cha","sequence":9750},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-pro","sequence":9870},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-use","sequence":9890},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-ses","sequence":9910},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-ses","sequence":9930},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-scr","sequence":9950},{"ref":"https://ns.cyber.gov.au/ism/set/tax/guidelines-for-system-hardening-authentication-hardening-log","sequence":9970}],"title":"Authentication hardening","version":"2026.06.18"}
