parts.prose: LAN Manager and NT LAN Manager authentication methods are disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security questions are not used for authentication purposes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email is not used for out-of-band authentication purposes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users to their organisation’s online services that process, store or communicate their organisation’s sensitive data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users to third-party online services that process, store or communicate their organisation’s sensitive data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication (where available) is used to authenticate users to third-party online services that process, store or communicate their organisation’s non-sensitive data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users to their organisation’s online customer services that process, store or communicate their organisation’s sensitive customer data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users to third-party online customer services that process, store or communicate their organisation’s sensitive customer data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate customers to online customer services that process, store or communicate sensitive customer data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users or customers to online services or online customer services, all other authentication protocols that do not support multi-factor authentication are disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate privileged users of systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate unprivileged users of systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users of data repositories.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication uses either: something users have and something users know, or something users have that is unlocked by something users know or are.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication used for authenticating users of online services is phishing-resistant.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication used for authenticating customers of online customer services provides a phishing-resistant option.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication used for authenticating customers of online customer services is phishing-resistant.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication used for authenticating users of systems is phishing-resistant.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Multi-factor authentication used for authenticating users of data repositories is phishing-resistant.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication is used by user accounts, other non-phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication options are disabled for such user accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When multi-factor authentication is used to authenticate users to online services, online customer services, systems or data repositories – that process, store or communicate their organisation’s sensitive data or sensitive customer data – users are prevented from self-enrolling into multi-factor authentication from untrustworthy devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Successful and unsuccessful multi-factor authentication events are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When systems cannot support multi-factor authentication, single-factor authentication using passwords is implemented instead.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Successful and unsuccessful single-factor authentication events are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Passwords used for multi-factor authentication on TOP SECRET systems are a minimum of 10 characters.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Passwords used for single-factor authentication on TOP SECRET systems are a minimum of 20 characters.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Passwords using a sequence of words for single-factor authentication are not constructed using:

- a list of categorised words
- a real sentence in a natural language
- song lyrics, movie or television show quotes, literature, or any other publicly available material
- less than 4 random words for non-classified, OFFICIAL: Sensitive and PROTECTED systems; 5 random words for SECRET systems; or 6 random words for TOP SECRET systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Passwords appearing in lists of commonly used passwords or lists of compromised passwords are not used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Maximum length limits for passwords are not less than 64 characters.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Password complexity requirements are not imposed for passwords.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All ASCII printable characters are supported for passwords.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users provide sufficient evidence to verify their identity when requesting new credentials.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials set for user accounts are randomly generated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials are provided to users via a secure communications channel or, if not possible, split into two parts with one part provided to users and the other part provided to supervisors.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials provided to users are changed on first use.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials are not reused by users across different systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for the built-in Administrator account in each domain are long, unique, unpredictable and managed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for break glass accounts, local administrator accounts and service accounts are long, unique, unpredictable and managed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for built-in Administrator accounts, break glass accounts, local administrator accounts and service accounts are a minimum of 30 characters.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for built-in Administrator accounts, break glass accounts, local administrator accounts and service accounts are randomly generated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Service accounts are created as group Managed Service Accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Credentials for user accounts are changed if:

- they are compromised
- they are suspected of being compromised
- they are discovered stored on networks in the clear
- they are discovered being transferred across networks in the clear
- membership of a shared user account changes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for computer accounts are changed if they are compromised, they are suspected of being compromised or they have not been changed in the past 30 days.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials for the Kerberos Key Distribution Center’s service account (KRBTGT) are changed twice, allowing for replication to all Microsoft AD DS domain controllers in-between each change, if the domain has been directly compromised, the domain is suspected of being compromised or they have not been changed in the past 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Microsoft AD FS token-signing and encryption certificates are changed twice in quick succession if they are compromised, they are suspected of being compromised or they have not been changed in the past 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials are obscured as they are entered into systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credential hint functionality is not used for systems.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Physical credentials are kept separate from systems they are used to authenticate to, except for when performing authentication activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credentials stored on systems are protected by a password manager; a hardware security module; or by salting, hashing and stretching them before storage within a database.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Private keys for Microsoft AD CS CA servers are protected by a hardware security module.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Memory integrity functionality is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Local Security Authority protection functionality is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Credential Guard functionality is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Remote Credential Guard functionality is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cached credentials are limited to one previous logon.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Networks are scanned at least monthly to identify any credentials that are being stored in the clear.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: User accounts, except for break glass accounts, are protected by fixed or risk-based lockout mechanisms aligned to a maximum of five failed logon attempts, with either indefinite or automated lockout durations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: User sessions are terminated and workstations are restarted at least daily.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Services are configured with a session lock that:

- activates after a maximum of 15 minutes of user inactivity, a maximum of 12 hours of overall session time or when manually activated by users
- blocks access to all session content
- requires users to re-authenticate using all authentication factors to unlock the session
- denies users the ability to disable the session locking mechanism.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Systems are configured with a screen lock that:

- activates after a maximum of 15 minutes of user inactivity, or when manually activated by users
- conceals all content on the screen
- ensures that the screen does not enter a power saving state before the screen lock is activated
- requires users to re-authenticate using all authentication factors to unlock the system
- denies users the ability to disable the screen locking mechanism.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Systems have a logon banner that reminds users of their security responsibilities when accessing the system and its resources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources, the isolation mechanism is from a vendor that has demonstrated a commitment to Secure by Design and Secure by Default principles and practices, including secure programming practices and either memory-safe programming languages or less preferably memory-safe programming practices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources, the configuration of the isolation mechanism is hardened by removing unneeded functionality and restricting access to the administrative interface used to manage the isolation mechanism.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources, the underlying operating system is hardened.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources, patches, updates or vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities are applied to the isolation mechanism and underlying operating system in a timely manner.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources, the isolation mechanism or underlying operating system is replaced when it is no longer supported by a vendor.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical resources, integrity monitoring and centralised event logging is performed for the isolation mechanism and underlying operating system.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using a software-based isolation mechanism that consumes shared physical computing resources for SECRET or TOP SECRET operating environments, the physical server and all operating environments are of the same classification and belong to the same security domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administration processes, and supporting system administration procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators perform system administration activities in accordance with the system’s change and configuration management plan.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secure Admin Workstations are used in the performance of administrative activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged users use separate privileged and unprivileged operating environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged operating environments are not virtualised within unprivileged operating environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unprivileged user accounts cannot logon to privileged operating environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged user accounts (excluding local administrator accounts) cannot logon to unprivileged operating environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: User accounts with DCSync permissions cannot logon to unprivileged operating environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Administrative infrastructure is segregated from the wider network and the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Administrative infrastructure for critical servers, high-value servers and regular servers is segregated from each other.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network management traffic can only originate from administrative infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Administrative activities are conducted through jump servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices that do not belong to administrative infrastructure cannot initiate connections with administrative infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software registers for workstations, servers, network devices and networked IT equipment are developed, implemented, maintained and regularly verified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software registers contain versions and patch histories of applications, drivers, operating systems and firmware.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patch management processes, and supporting patch management procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A centralised and managed approach that maintains the integrity of patches or updates, and confirms that they have been applied successfully, is used to patch or update applications, operating systems, drivers and firmware.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in online services are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in online services are applied within two weeks of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products are applied within two weeks of release.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products are applied within two weeks of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in applications other than office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products are applied within one month of release.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of internet-facing servers and internet-facing network devices are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of internet-facing servers and internet-facing network devices are applied within two weeks of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of workstations, non-internet-facing servers and non-internet-facing network devices are applied within one month of release.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of workstations, non-internet-facing servers and non-internet-facing network devices are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of workstations, non-internet-facing servers and non-internet-facing network devices are applied within one month of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of IT equipment other than workstations, servers and network devices are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in operating systems of IT equipment other than workstations, servers and network devices are applied within one month of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in drivers are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in drivers are applied within one month of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in firmware are applied within 48 hours of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as critical by vendors or when working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in firmware are applied within one month of release when vulnerabilities are assessed as non-critical by vendors and no working exploits exist.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Patches, updates or other vendor mitigations for vulnerabilities in high assurance IT equipment are applied only when approved by ASD, and in doing so, using methods and timeframes prescribed by ASD.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Online services that are no longer supported by vendors are removed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, Adobe Flash Player, and security products that are no longer supported by vendors are removed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Applications other than office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, Adobe Flash Player, and security products that are no longer supported by vendors are removed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Operating systems that are no longer supported by vendors are replaced.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Internet-facing network devices that are no longer supported by vendors are replaced.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Non-internet-facing network devices that are no longer supported by vendors are replaced.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Networked IT equipment that is no longer supported by vendors is replaced.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When applications, operating systems, network devices or networked IT equipment that are no longer supported by vendors cannot be immediately removed or replaced, compensating controls are implemented until such time that they can be removed or replaced.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A digital preservation policy is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data backup processes, and supporting data backup procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data restoration processes, and supporting data restoration procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Backups of data, applications and settings are performed and retained in accordance with business criticality and business continuity requirements.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Backups of data, applications and settings are synchronised to enable restoration to a common point in time.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Backups of data, applications and settings are retained in a secure and resilient manner.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unprivileged user accounts cannot access backups belonging to other user accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unprivileged user accounts cannot access their own backups.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged user accounts (excluding backup administrator accounts) cannot access backups belonging to other user accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged user accounts (excluding backup administrator accounts) cannot access their own backups.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unprivileged user accounts are prevented from modifying and deleting backups.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Privileged user accounts (excluding backup administrator accounts) are prevented from modifying and deleting backups.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Backup administrator accounts are prevented from modifying and deleting backups during their retention period.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Restoration of data, applications and settings from backups to a common point in time is tested as part of disaster recovery exercises.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A security monitoring policy is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A centralised event logging facility is implemented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs sent to a centralised event logging facility are sent as soon as possible after they occur.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs sent to a centralised event logging facility are encrypted in transit using Australian Signals Directorate (ASD)-approved cryptography.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs are protected from unauthorised access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs are protected from unauthorised modification and deletion.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An accurate and consistent time source is used for event logging.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: For each event logged, the date and time of the event, the relevant user or process, the relevant filename, the event description, and the information technology equipment involved are captured.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: To the extent possible, event logs are captured and stored in a consistent and structured format.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cyber security personnel have access to sufficient tools to facilitate the detection of cyber security events and the identification of cyber security incidents.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cyber threat intelligence services are used to support the detection of cyber security events and the identification of cyber security incidents.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Suitable AI models are used to augment the detection of cyber security events and the identification of cyber security incidents.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from critical servers are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from internet-facing servers are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from non-internet-facing servers are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from workstations are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from security products are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from internet-facing network devices are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs from non-internet-facing network devices are analysed in a timely manner to detect cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cyber security events are analysed in a timely manner to identify cyber security incidents.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs are retained in a searchable manner for at least 12 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs are retained as per minimum retention requirements for various classes of records as set out by the National Archives of Australia’s Administrative Functions Disposal Authority Express (AFDA Express) Version 2 publication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An automated method of asset discovery is used at least fortnightly to support the detection of assets for subsequent vulnerability scanning activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner with an up-to-date vulnerability database is used for vulnerability scanning activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least daily to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in online services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least weekly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least fortnightly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in applications other than office productivity suites, web browsers and their extensions, email clients, PDF applications, and security products.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least daily to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in operating systems of internet-facing servers and internet-facing network devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least fortnightly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in operating systems of workstations, non-internet-facing servers and non-internet-facing network devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least fortnightly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in operating systems of IT equipment other than workstations, servers and network devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least fortnightly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in drivers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability scanner is used at least fortnightly to identify missing patches or updates for vulnerabilities in firmware.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The likelihood of system compromise is frequently assessed when working exploits exist for unmitigated vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Vulnerability assessments and penetration tests are conducted for systems prior to their deployment, including prior to the deployment of significant changes, and at least annually thereafter.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Suitable AI models are used to augment vulnerability assessments and penetration tests.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Development, testing, staging and production environments are segregated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Development and modification of software only take place in development environments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data from production environments is not used in non-production environments unless the non-production environment is secured to at least the same level as the production environment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An authoritative source for software is established and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The authoritative source for software is used for all software development activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unauthorised access to the authoritative source for software is prevented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unauthorised modification of the authoritative source for software is prevented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An issue tracking solution is used to link software development tasks to security issues and decisions, change or feature requests, programming issues, or bug fixes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All software artefacts are scanned for malicious content before being imported into the authoritative source for software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All software artefacts are verified by a digital signature, or a secure hash provided over a secure channel, before being imported into the authoritative source for software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All software artefacts are tested to detect known weaknesses using static application security testing (SAST), dynamic application security testing (DAST) or software composition analysis (SCA), depending on the software artefact type, before being imported into the authoritative source for software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Existing software artefacts in the authoritative source for software are periodically tested to detect known weaknesses using SAST, DAST or SCA, depending on the software artefact type, throughout the software development life cycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The authoritative source for software restricts the use and import of third-party libraries and software components to trustworthy sources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Scanning is used during commits to identify plain text or encoded secrets and keys, which are then blocked from being stored in the authoritative source for software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Compilers, interpreters and build tools (including pipelines) that provide security features to improve executable file security are implemented and such security features are used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The build solution ensures that all automated testing is completed without warnings, alerts or errors before building software artefacts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A secure software development policy is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All software security requirements are documented, stored securely and maintained throughout the software development life cycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security design decisions are documented and reviewed throughout the software development cycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security roles, responsibilities and knowledge required to support the software development life cycle are identified and documented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security responsibilities for software developers are identified and documented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software developers that lack sufficient cyber security knowledge and skills required for their projects or tasks are not used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software developers that lack sufficient cyber security knowledge and skills required for their projects or tasks undertake suitable training or upskilling on secure software development and programming practices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A software developer cyber security knowledge and skills register is implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secure by Design principles and practices are followed throughout the software development life cycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Threat modelling is used in support of the software development life cycle.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The software threat model is reviewed throughout the software development life cycle to ensure it reflects the as-built software and any changes to the threat environment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secure programming practices for the chosen programming language are used for software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Memory-safe programming languages, or less preferably memory-safe programming practices, are used for software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secure by Default principles and practices are followed throughout the software development life cycle, including by ensuring that all built-in security measures are included and enabled in the base product at no extra cost to consumers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SecDevOps practices are used for software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software is architected and structured to support readability and maintainability.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Files containing executable content are digitally signed by a certificate with a verifiable chain of trust as part of software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Installers, patches and updates are digitally signed or provided with cryptographic checksums as part of software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software has no default credentials; however, if credentials are required, they are created on first install by the installing organisation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Application backwards compatibility does not compromise any security measures or features.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where software allows user impersonation, sensitive data is not logged and appropriate permissions are set.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where software allows an authentication factor to be reset, the user is notified of the reset through a secondary channel.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where software supports multiple user roles, non-administrative users are prevented from altering their profile permissions or privileges.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When user permissions or credentials are changed, software forces all impacted users to re-authenticate.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When digital signatures are processed by software, they are validated against a certificate trust chain and checked for revocation using a Certificate Revocation List or with the Online Certificate Status Protocol.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software generates sufficient event logs to support the detection of cyber security events.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Event logs produced by software ensure that any sensitive data is protected.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Secure configuration guidance, in the form of a hardening guide or loosening guide, is produced and made available to consumers as part of software development.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: End of life procedures for software, including procedures for software removal and the archival or destruction of user accounts and data, are produced and made available to consumers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If a software bill of materials is available for imported third-party software components, it is used during software development to ensure such software components have no known vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A software bill of materials is produced and made available to consumers of software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If a cryptographic bill of materials is available for imported third-party software components, it is used during software development to ensure such software components provide support for standardised implementations of ASD-Approved Cryptographic Algorithms.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A cryptographic bill of materials is produced and made available to consumers of software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If a software build provenance is available for imported third-party software components, it is used during software development to ensure such software components are built to an appropriate standard.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A software build provenance is produced and made available to consumers of software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Authentication and authorisation of clients is performed when clients call network APIs that facilitate modification of data and are accessible over the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Authentication and authorisation of clients is performed when clients call network APIs that facilitate modification of data but are not accessible over the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Authentication and authorisation of clients is performed when clients call network APIs that facilitate access to data not authorised for release into the public domain and are accessible over the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Authentication and authorisation of clients is performed when clients call network APIs that facilitate access to data not authorised for release into the public domain but are not accessible over the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network API calls that facilitate modification of data, or access to data not authorised for release into the public domain, and are accessible over the internet, are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network API calls that facilitate modification of data, or access to data not authorised for release into the public domain, but are not accessible over the internet, are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Validation and sanitisation are performed on all input received over the internet by software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Validation and sanitisation are performed on all input received over a local network by software.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All input validation rules are documented, implemented in code, and tested using both positive and negative unit tests and integration tests.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data sources and serialised data inputs are validated before being deserialised.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: File uploads or input are restricted to specific file types, with malicious content scanning occurring prior to file access, file execution or file storage.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All queries to databases from software are filtered for legitimate content and correct syntax.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Parameterised queries or stored procedures, instead of dynamically generated queries, are used by software for database interactions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software is designed or configured to provide as little error information as possible about the structure of databases.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All queries to databases from software that are initiated by users, and any resulting crash or error messages, are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Peer reviews are conducted on all critical and security-related software components.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Code reviews are utilised to ensure software components meets Secure by Design principles and practices as well as secure programming practices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unit testing and integration testing, covering both positive and negative use cases, are used for software components to ensure code quality and correctness.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Software is comprehensively tested for vulnerabilities using SAST, DAST and SCA prior to its initial release, any subsequent release, and periodically to help identify any previously unidentified vulnerabilities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Suitable AI models are used to augment software security testing.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability disclosure program is implemented to assist with the secure development and maintenance of products and services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A vulnerability disclosure policy is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Vulnerability disclosure processes, and supporting vulnerability disclosure procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A ‘security.txt’ file is hosted for each of an organisation’s internet-facing website domains to assist in the responsible disclosure of vulnerabilities in the organisation’s products and services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Vulnerabilities identified in software are publicly disclosed in a responsible and timely manner, including with Common Weakness Enumeration and Common Platform Enumeration information.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Vulnerabilities identified in software are resolved in a timely manner.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: In resolving vulnerabilities, root cause analysis is performed and, to the greatest extent possible, entire vulnerability classes are remediated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security-relevant usage, error messages and crashes for software are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI-specific documentation, including AI model cards and AI system cards (or equivalent artefacts), is used to document AI model characteristics, system architectures, use cases and security risks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI models are stored in a non-executable file format that does not allow arbitrary code execution.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The exposure of exact AI model confidence scores in API outputs or user interfaces is prevented.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Organisational data generated, collected or processed by AI applications is not used for training, fine-tuning or improving AI models unless informed and explicit consent has been obtained from data owners in advance.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All prompts and outputs associated with chat sessions are securely deleted when chat sessions are removed from AI applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The source and integrity of AI models, structures and weights are verified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The source and integrity of training data for AI models is verified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data validation and verification techniques are used to ensure the reliability and accuracy of training data used by AI models.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI model performance metrics are monitored and anomalies are investigated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Rate limiting is applied to inference queries for AI models.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Resource limits are enforced for AI models.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access control policies are implemented to enforce fine-grained permissions for AI applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Role-based access controls are implemented for AI applications to restrict access to sensitive data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Generative AI applications evaluate user prompts to detect and mitigate adversarial inputs or suffixes designed to elicit unintended behaviour or assist in the generation of sensitive or harmful content.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Content filtering is implemented by AI applications to detect and block sensitive data exposure and improper output.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard is used in the development of mobile applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Robust web application frameworks are used in the development of web applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The OWASP Application Security Verification Standard is used in the development of web applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The OWASP Top 10 Proactive Controls are used in the development of web applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The OWASP Top 10 are mitigated in the development of web applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If supported, web application session cookies set the HttpOnly flag, Secure flag and the SameSite flag by default.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Web application session cookies contain only digitally signed opaque bearer tokens.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Web application session cookies using opaque bearer tokens that are not digitally signed use non-sequential random identifiers with a minimum of 128 bits of entropy, preferably 256 bits of entropy.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Web application sessions are centrally managed server side.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Web applications that support Single Sign On equally support Single Logout.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Content-Security-Policy, Hypertext Transfer Protocol Strict Transport Security and X-Frame-Options are specified by web server software via security policy in response headers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All web application content is offered exclusively using HTTPS.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The OWASP API Security Top 10 are mitigated in the development of web APIs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Output encoding is performed on all output produced by web applications.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Database servers and web servers are functionally separated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Data communicated between database servers and web servers is encrypted using Australian Signals Directorate-approved cryptography.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Database servers are placed on a different network segment to user workstations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network access controls are implemented to restrict database server communications to strictly defined network resources that require access to the database server.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If only local access to a database is required, networking functionality of database management system applications is disabled or directed to listen solely to the localhost interface.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Database servers for development, testing, staging and production environments are segregated.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A database register is developed, implemented, maintained and regularly verified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: File-based access controls are applied to database files.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Databases and their contents are classified based on the sensitivity or classification of data that they contain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Database users’ ability to access, insert, modify and remove database contents is restricted based on their work duties.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The need-to-know principle is enforced for database contents through the application of minimum privileges, database views, database roles and data tokenisation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Database contents from production environments are not used in non-production environments unless the non-production environment is secured to at least the same level as the production environment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Security-relevant events for databases are centrally logged, including:

- access or modification of particularly important content
- addition of new users, especially privileged users
- changes to user roles or privileges
- attempts to elevate user privileges
- queries containing comments
- queries containing multiple embedded queries
- database and query alerts or failures
- database structure changes
- database administrator actions
- use of executable commands
- database logons and logoffs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An email usage policy is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access to non-approved webmail services is blocked.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Protective markings are applied to emails and reflect the highest sensitivity or classification of the subject, body and attachments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Protective marking tools do not automatically insert protective markings into emails.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Protective marking tools do not allow users to select protective markings that a system has not been authorised to process, store or communicate.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Protective marking tools do not allow users replying to or forwarding emails to select protective markings lower than previously used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email servers are configured to block, log and report emails with inappropriate protective markings.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The intended recipients of blocked inbound emails, and the senders of blocked outbound emails, are notified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Emails containing Australian Eyes Only, Australian Government Access Only or Releasable To data are not sent to email distribution lists unless the nationality of all members of email distribution lists can be confirmed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Emails are routed via centralised email gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When users send or receive emails, an authenticated and encrypted channel is used to route emails via their organisation’s centralised email gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where backup or alternative email gateways are in place, they are maintained at the same standard as the primary email gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email servers only relay emails destined for or originating from their domains (including subdomains).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Opportunistic TLS encryption is enabled on email servers that make incoming or outgoing email connections over public network infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MTA-STS is enabled to prevent the unencrypted transfer of emails between email servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SPF is used to specify authorised email servers (or lack thereof) for an organisation’s domains (including subdomains).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A hard fail SPF record is used when specifying authorised email servers (or lack thereof) for an organisation’s domains (including subdomains).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SPF is used to verify the authenticity of incoming emails.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DKIM signing is enabled on emails originating from an organisation’s domains (including subdomains).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DKIM signatures on incoming emails are verified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email distribution list applications used by external senders is configured such that it does not break the validity of the sender’s DKIM signature.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DMARC records are configured for an organisation’s domains (including subdomains) such that emails are rejected if they do not pass DMARC checks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Incoming emails are rejected if they do not pass DMARC checks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Email content filtering is implemented to filter potentially harmful content in email bodies and attachments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Emails arriving via an external connection where the email source address uses an internal domain, or internal subdomain, are blocked at the email gateway.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Notifications of undeliverable emails are only sent to senders that can be verified via SPF or other trusted means.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network documentation is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network documentation includes high-level network diagrams showing all connections into networks and logical network diagrams showing all critical servers, high-value servers, network devices and network security appliances.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network documentation includes device settings for all critical servers, high-value servers, network devices and network security appliances.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network documentation provided to a third party, or published in public tender documentation, only contains details necessary for other parties to undertake contractual services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Networks are segregated into multiple network zones according to the criticality of servers, services and data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An organisation’s networks are segregated from their service providers’ networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VLANs are not used to separate network traffic between an organisation’s networks and public network infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: VLANs are not used to separate network traffic between networks belonging to different security domains.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices managing VLANs are administered from the most trusted security domain.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices managing VLANs belonging to different security domains do not share VLAN trunks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices managing VLANs terminate VLANs belonging to different security domains on separate physical network interfaces.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Internet connectivity for networked devices is strictly limited to those that require access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Networked management interfaces for IT equipment are not directly exposed to the internet.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Servers maintain effective functional separation from each other.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Servers minimise communications with other servers at the network and file system level.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All data communicated over network infrastructure is encrypted using ASD-approved cryptography.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IPv6 functionality is disabled in dual-stack network devices unless it is being used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IPv6 capable network security appliances are used on IPv6 and dual-stack networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unless explicitly required, IPv6 tunnelling is disabled on all network devices.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IPv6 tunnelling is blocked by network security appliances at externally connected network boundaries.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Dynamically assigned IPv6 addresses are configured with Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol version 6 in a stateful manner with lease data stored in a centralised event logging facility.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network access controls are implemented on networks to prevent the connection of unauthorised network devices and networked IT equipment.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network access controls are implemented to limit the flow of network traffic within and between network segments to only that required for business purposes.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security measures are implemented to prevent unauthorised access to network management traffic.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SMB version 1 is not used on networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SNMP version 1 and SNMP version 2 are not used on networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All default SNMP community strings on network devices are changed and write access is disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A NIDS or NIPS is deployed in gateways between an organisation’s networks and other networks they do not manage.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A NIDS or NIPS is located immediately inside the outermost firewall for gateways and configured to generate event logs and alerts for network traffic that contravenes any rule in a firewall ruleset.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Inbound network connections from anonymity networks are blocked.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Outbound network connections to anonymity networks are blocked.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DNS traffic is encrypted by clients and servers using ASD-approved cryptography.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A protective DNS service is used to block access to known malicious domain names.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices are flashed with trusted firmware before they are used for the first time.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Default user accounts or credentials for network devices, including for any pre-configured user accounts, are changed, disabled or removed during initial setup.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Unused physical ports on network devices are disabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Network devices are restarted at least monthly.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security-relevant events for internet-facing network devices are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security-relevant events for non-internet-facing network devices are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All wireless devices are Wi-Fi Alliance certified.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Public wireless networks provided for public use are segregated from all other organisation networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The administrative interface on wireless access points is disabled for wireless network connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Settings for wireless access points are hardened.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Default SSIDs of wireless access points are changed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SSIDs of non-public wireless networks are not readily associated with an organisation, the location of their premises or the functionality of wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SSID broadcasting is not disabled on wireless access points.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MAC address filtering is not used to restrict which devices can connect to wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Static addressing is not used for assigning IP addresses on wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode is used to protect the confidentiality and integrity of all wireless network traffic.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: 802.1X authentication with EAP-TLS, using X.509 certificates, is used for mutual authentication; with all other EAP methods disabled on supplicants and authentication servers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: User identity confidentiality is used if available with EAP-TLS implementations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated supplicants, authenticators, wireless access points and authentication servers are used in wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Certificates are generated using an evaluated certificate authority or hardware security module.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Certificates are required for devices and users accessing wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Certificates are protected by logical and physical access controls, encryption, and user authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The PMK caching period is not set to greater than 1440 minutes (24 hours).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The use of FT (802.11r) is disabled unless authenticator-to-authenticator communications are secured by an ASD-Approved Cryptographic Protocol.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Communications between authenticators and a RADIUS server are encapsulated with an additional layer of encryption using RADIUS over Internet Protocol Security or RADIUS over Transport Layer Security.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless networks implement sufficient frequency separation from other wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Wireless access points enable the use of the 802.11w amendment to protect management frames.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Instead of deploying a small number of wireless access points that broadcast on high power, a greater number of wireless access points that use less broadcast power are deployed to achieve the desired footprint for wireless networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The effective range of wireless communications outside an organisation’s area of control is limited by implementing RF shielding on facilities in which SECRET or TOP SECRET wireless networks are used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cloud service providers are used for hosting online services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cloud service providers’ ability to scale resources dynamically in response to genuine spikes in demand is discussed and verified as part of capacity and availability planning for online services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where a high availability requirement exists for online services, the services are architected to automatically transition between availability zones.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Continuous real-time monitoring of the capacity and availability of online services is performed.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where a high availability requirement exists for website hosting, CDNs that cache websites are used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If using CDNs, disclosing the IP addresses of web servers under an organisation’s control (referred to as origin servers) is avoided and access to the origin servers is restricted to the CDNs and authorised management networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Denial-of-service attack mitigation strategies are discussed with cloud service providers, specifically:

- their capacity to withstand denial-of-service attacks
- costs likely to be incurred as a result of denial-of-service attacks
- availability monitoring and thresholds for notification of denial-of-service attacks
- thresholds for turning off any online services or functionality during denial-of-service attacks
- pre-approved actions that can be undertaken during denial-of-service attacks
- any arrangements with upstream service providers to block malicious network traffic as far upstream as possible.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Critical online services are segregated from other online services that are more likely to be targeted as part of denial-of-service attacks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Domain names for online services are protected via registrar locking and confirming that domain registration details are correct.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Communications security doctrine and policy produced by ASD for the management and operation of HACE is complied with.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: HACE are issued an Approval for Use by ASD and operated in accordance with the latest version of their associated Australian Communications Security Instructions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Cryptographic key management processes, and supporting cryptographic key management procedures, are developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: HACE is used when encrypting media that contains SECRET or TOP SECRET data.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: HACE is used to protect SECRET and TOP SECRET data when communicated over insufficiently secure networks, outside of appropriately secure areas or via public network infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where practical, cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries provide a means of data recovery to allow for circumstances where the encryption key is unavailable due to loss, damage or failure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When a user authenticates to the encryption functionality of IT equipment or media, it is treated in accordance with its original sensitivity or classification until the user deauthenticates from the encryption functionality.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Keyed cryptographic equipment is transported based on the sensitivity or classification of its keying material.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The compromise or suspected compromise of cryptographic equipment or associated keying material is reported to the chief information security officer, or one of their delegates, as soon as possible after it occurs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Keying material is changed when compromised or suspected of being compromised.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An AACA or high assurance cryptographic algorithm is used when encrypting data at rest.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Only AACAs or high assurance cryptographic algorithms are used by cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: ECDH is used in preference to DH.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using DH for agreeing on encryption session keys, a modulus of at least 3072 bits is used, preferably 3072 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using DH for agreeing on encryption session keys, a modulus and associated parameters are selected according to NIST SP 800-56A Rev. 3.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using elliptic curve cryptography, a suitable curve from NIST SP 800-186 is used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ECDH for agreeing on encryption session keys, NIST P-384 or P-521 curves are used, preferably the NIST P-384 curve.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ECDSA for digital signatures, NIST P-384 or P-521 curves are used, preferably the NIST P-384 curve.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ML-DSA and ML-KEM, as per FIPS 204 and FIPS 203 respectively, adherence to pre-requisite FIPS 140-3 validation is preferred.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ML-DSA for digital signatures, ML-DSA-65 or ML-DSA-87 is used, preferably ML-DSA-87.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ML-DSA for digital signatures, the hedged variant is used whenever possible.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Pre-hashed variants of ML-DSA-65 and ML-DSA-87 are only used when the performance of default variants is unacceptable.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When the pre-hashed variants of ML-DSA-65 and ML-DSA-87 are used, at least SHA-384 and SHA-512 respectively are used for pre-hashing.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using ML-KEM for encapsulating encryption session keys (and similar keys), ML-KEM-768 or ML-KEM-1024 is used, preferably ML-KEM-1024.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using RSA for digital signatures, and transporting encryption session keys (and similar keys), a modulus of at least 3072 bits is used, preferably 3072 bits.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using RSA for digital signatures, and for transporting encryption session keys (and similar keys), a different key pair is used for digital signatures and transporting encryption session keys.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using SHA-2 for hashing, an output size of at least 384 bits is used, preferably SHA-384 or SHA-512.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using AES for encryption, AES-192 or AES-256 is used, preferably AES-256.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Symmetric cryptographic algorithms are not used in Electronic Codebook Mode.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A post-quantum cryptography transition plan is developed, implemented and maintained.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The development and procurement of new cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries ensures support for the use of ML-DSA-87, ML-KEM-1024, SHA-384, SHA-512 and AES-256 by no later than 2030.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When a post-quantum traditional hybrid scheme is used, either the post-quantum cryptographic algorithm, the traditional cryptographic algorithm or both are AACAs.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: An AACP or high assurance cryptographic protocol is used when encrypting data in transit.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Only AACPs or high assurance cryptographic protocols are used by cryptographic equipment, applications and libraries.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Only the latest version of TLS is used for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AES-GCM is used for encryption of TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Only server-initiated secure renegotiation is used for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DH or ECDH is used for key establishment of TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When using DH or ECDH for key establishment of TLS connections, the ephemeral variant is used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Anonymous DH is not used for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SHA-2-based certificates are used for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SHA-2 is used for the Hash-based Message Authentication Code (HMAC) and pseudorandom function (PRF) for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: TLS compression is disabled for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) is used for TLS connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The use of SSH version 1 is disabled for SSH connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
The SSH daemon is configured to:

- only listen on the required interfaces (ListenAddress xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx)
- have a suitable login banner (Banner x)
- have a login authentication timeout of no more than 60 seconds (LoginGraceTime 60)
- disable host-based authentication (HostbasedAuthentication no)
- disable rhosts-based authentication (IgnoreRhosts yes)
- disable the ability to log in directly as root (PermitRootLogin no)
- disable empty passwords (PermitEmptyPasswords no)
- disable connection forwarding (AllowTCPForwarding no)
- disable gateway ports (GatewayPorts no)
- disable X11 forwarding (X11Forwarding no).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Public key-based authentication is used for SSH connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: SSH private keys are protected with a password or a key encryption key.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
When using logins without a password for SSH connections, the following are disabled:

- access from IP addresses that do not require access
- port forwarding
- agent credential forwarding
- X11 forwarding
- console access.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If using remote access without the use of a password for SSH connections, the ‘forced command’ option is used to specify what command is executed and parameter checking is enabled.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When SSH-agent or similar key caching applications are used, it is limited to workstations and servers with screen locks and key caches that are set to expire within four hours of inactivity.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Versions of S/MIME earlier than S/MIME version 3.0 are not used for S/MIME connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Tunnel mode is used for IPsec connections; however, if using transport mode, an IP tunnel is used.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The ESP protocol is used for authentication and encryption of IPsec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IKE version 2 is used for key exchange when establishing IPsec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AES is used for encrypting IPsec connections, preferably ENCR_AES_GCM_16.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: PRF_HMAC_SHA2_256, PRF_HMAC_SHA2_384 or PRF_HMAC_SHA2_512 is used for IPsec connections, preferably PRF_HMAC_SHA2_512.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AUTH_HMAC_SHA2_256_128, AUTH_HMAC_SHA2_384_192, AUTH_HMAC_SHA2_512_256 or NONE (only with AES-GCM) is used for authenticating IPsec connections, preferably NONE.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: DH or ECDH is used for key establishment of IPsec connections, preferably 384-bit random ECP group, 3072-bit MODP Group or 4096-bit MODP Group.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A security association lifetime of less than four hours (14400 seconds) is used for IPsec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: PFS is used for IPsec connections.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways are implemented between networks belonging to different security domains.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways implement a demilitarised zone if external parties require access to an organisation’s services.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways only allow explicitly authorised data flows.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways inspect and filter data flows at the transport and above network layers.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways perform ingress traffic filtering to detect and prevent IP source address spoofing.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators for gateways undergo appropriate employment screening, and where necessary hold an appropriate security clearance, based on the sensitivity or classification of gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators for gateways that connect to Australian Eyes Only or Releasable To networks are Australian nationals.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators for gateways that connect to Australian Government Access Only networks are Australian nationals or seconded foreign nationals.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators for gateways are assigned the minimum privileges required to perform their duties.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Separation of duties is implemented in performing administrative activities for gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: System administrators for gateways are formally trained on the operation and management of gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways are managed via a secure path isolated from all connected networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: For gateways between networks belonging to different security domains, any shared components are managed by system administrators for the higher security domain or by system administrators from a mutually agreed upon third party.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users authenticate to other networks accessed via gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: IT equipment authenticates to other networks accessed via gateways.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Public IP addresses controlled by, or used by, an organisation are signed by valid ROA records.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Routes for RPKI-registered IP addresses that are advertised from invalid Autonomous Systems, or that are longer than allowed, are rejected or deprioritised by routers that exchange routes via BGP.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
Security-relevant events for gateways are centrally logged, including:

- data packets and data flows permitted through gateways
- data packets and data flows attempting to leave gateways
- real-time alerts for attempted intrusions.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Gateways undergo testing following configuration changes, and at regular intervals no more than six months apart, to validate that they conform to expected security configurations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: TOP SECRET gateways undergo a security assessment by ASD assessors (or their delegates), using the latest release of the ISM available prior to the beginning of the assessment (or a subsequent release), at least every 24 months.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CDSs are implemented between SECRET or TOP SECRET networks and any other networks belonging to different security domains.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: When planning, designing, implementing or introducing additional connectivity to CDSs, ASD is consulted and any directions provided by ASD are complied with.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CDSs implement isolated upward and downward network paths.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CDSs implement independent security-enforcing functions for upward and downward network paths.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: CDSs implement protocol breaks at each network layer.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Security-relevant events for CDSs are centrally logged.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: A sample of security-relevant events relating to data transfer policies are taken at least every three months and assessed against security policies for CDSs to identify any operational failures.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Users are trained on the secure use of CDSs before access is granted.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated firewalls are used between an organisation’s networks and public network infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated firewalls are used between networks belonging to different security domains.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If using a WAF, disclosing the IP addresses of web servers under an organisation’s control (referred to as origin servers) is avoided and access to the origin servers is restricted to the WAF and authorised management networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated diodes are used for controlling the data flow of unidirectional gateways between an organisation’s networks and public network infrastructure.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated diodes used for controlling the data flow of unidirectional gateways between SECRET or TOP SECRET networks and public network infrastructure complete a high assurance evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated diodes are used for controlling the data flow of unidirectional gateways between networks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Evaluated diodes used for controlling the data flow of unidirectional gateways between SECRET or TOP SECRET networks and any other networks complete a high assurance evaluation.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All web access, including that by internal servers, is conducted through web proxies.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose:
The following details are centrally logged for websites accessed via web proxies:

- web address
- date and time
- user
- amount of data uploaded and downloaded
- internal and external IP addresses.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Web content filtering is implemented to filter potentially harmful web-based content.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Client-side active content is restricted by web content filters to an organisation-approved list of domain names.

parts.name: statement
